File.Name: 4c18b_4c18-066.jpg
Notes: 9074588 Looking into the coiled new tendril of a native Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) in the woods with afternoon light. This is a trailing or climbing perennial vine that dies back and grows anew every year from a huge underground tuber. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c18c_4c18-029.jpg
Notes: 9074534 Cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing at the base of a standing dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. They always grow on dead wood, sometimes buried. The mushrooms are poisonous. They're orange like the delicious Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), but you will never confuse them after you've seen them both. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)
ScientificName: Omphalotus olivascens; Quercus agrifolia; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Omphalotus olivascens; Quercus agrifolia; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: 4c18d_4c18-014.jpg
Notes: 9074464 Every year is different... This time we've had early rain, then dry, then a bit more rain, and now it's beautiful and dry all week. We have mushrooms, but they've been hiding under the crunchy leaves. When the mushrooms finally come up, they lift a "mushrump" of leaves and soil over their caps. These 'shrooms are Entoloma rhodopolium or kin (maybe E. clypeatum?) pushing up through the oak leaves. You can see some pink- or flesh-colored spores deposited on the lower mushroom cap. They have a "farinaceous" oder, like linseed oil. Don't eat them! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c18e_4c18-043.jpg
Notes: 9074417 Oyster Mushrooms (Pleurotis ostreatus) growing on a log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon near the creek. They are growing over sycamore and bay leaves and dusting them with white spores. Edible and delicious, the fan shape is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c18f_4c18-002.jpg
Notes: 9074367 Remember the huge cascade with 100s of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) that I posted week ago at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9023861)? This is all that's left today - a pool of slime crowded with semi-aquatic fly (Diptera) maggots swimming through the mess that smells like a hamper full of diapers with lots of ammonia. That's destiny - "Ars longa, Vita brevis" - flower while you can! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19j_4c19-085.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 9325423 Tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae). I twirl my beard too when I'm thinking or dreaming! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19i_4c19-094.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 9325409 We're heading into winter and insects are scarce in the canyon. But I caught this Flower Fly (Syrphidae) resting on a leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19h_4c18-045.jpg
Notes:
[mykologos] 10265261 Looking under the skirts at the soft white gills of an Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotis ostreatus) growing on a log of You know those tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams - Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats and lots more. I put my camera on a tripod today with manual focus and stood in the shade pointing towards the sun. I tried *lots* of different exposures to capture the light from their flight. This is 1/2 sec @ f/25 @ ISO 200, no flash.

When I first saw the double trails, I figured I was jittery from too much coffee. Looking closer, I think I understand. The tiny flies reflect sunlight from each wing with every wing beat. Depending on their path, they make double trails or those fanastic "snake-vertebrae" trails like little moving strobe lights. Knowing shutter speed and lens magnification, I could learn a lot from these photos - like how fast they move and beat their wings, and maybe what the heck they are up to. What's up with that "tuning fork" shape in the center!? Did two become one? Sounds like a science fair project! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

I have another photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9074417
CommonNames: Oyster Mushroom; Dance Flies; Fungus Gnats

File.Name: 4c19g_4c19-077.jpg
Notes:
[mykologos] 10265196 I'm not sure what this beautiful cluster of mushrooms is growing from an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump - maybe Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea)? (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

There's another photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=9080277

File.Name: 4c19a_4c19-073.jpg
Notes: 9080277 I'm not sure what this beautiful cluster of mushrooms is, but some woodland critter decided to take a nibble anyway. The 'shrooms might be Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) since they're growing from an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump. The caps are fibrillose, but the shape doesn't seem right. The caps do have a "partial veil" covering the gills that will become a ring on the stems. The veils haven't opened yet, but you can look through the critter-opening to see what a partial veil looks like from the inside. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19b_4c19-081.jpg
Notes: 9080153 Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a stump of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in a place where the sun never shines. I have to brag - the exposure is 4 sec @ f/16 @ ISO 200, no flash, using tripod and self-timer to trigger the shot. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19c_4c19-078.jpg
Notes: 9080109 A cascade of Entoloma rhodopolium (or kin) mushrooms growing in the woods. The classic way to identify mushrooms is by the color of the spore print - set the cap on paper to release spores overnight and check the color of the spore deposit. These mushrooms have made a natural spore print from top to bottom. This color is called "pink" in the books, but it is really more skin or cinnamon colored. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19d_4c19-040.jpg
Notes: 9080026 This pretty little Puffball (Lycoperdon) is releasng spores through the apical pore - that hole on top. You can tap the mushroom body and watch a puff of spores come out like smoke. The spore mass inside looks like that stuff in your vacuum cleaner bag. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19e_4c19-088.jpg
Notes: 9079924 One of our first Russula mushrooms pushing up through the leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This cap is just a bit pink around the edges, though other species are bright red. The ground is still damp from early rains, but the air is dry and the leaves are getting crunchy. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c19f_4c19-083.jpg
Notes: 9079846 "Are you looking at me?" It was spooky pointing my camera at this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) because it looked right at me and made eye contact! It has ruby eyes in this photo, but in another it has emerald eyes - I reckon it's one of those reflection/refraction things. Insects and spiders are scarce in the woods, but we've had a week of warm days to bring them out. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c20a_4c19-102.jpg
Notes:
9087844 Little Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) catching some rays to warm up in the garden. Think about it - sense organs are tricky for critters like lizards that must shed their skin as they grow! This photo shows eyes, nostrils, and an ear. What are those white "boogers" on its nose? Even more remarkable is the yellowish "third eye" clearly visible here toward the back of its head. See my [Previous] photo for another view. Try a google search for:
   http://www.google.com/search?q=%22western+fence+lizard%22+%22third+eye%22
to find interesting links ranging from good science to wacky. It`s so interesting that Descartes picked the pineal gland as the seat of the soul! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c20b_4c19-108.jpg
Notes: 9087674 I`ve heard that Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) have a "third eye", but I don`t think I ever saw it before today. This little lizard was trying to get warm on the side of our house. Sun is scarce as we approach the winter solstice here on the north-facing side of the mountains, so this critter let me get close. I was trying to focus on its other two eyes. I almost deleted this photo for being "too contrasty" - then I noticed the third eye while looking at my computer screen. There does seem to be a transparent cuticle or lens in the center. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c20c_4c19-111.jpg
Notes:
9087368 Notice the sharp little claws - so that's how lizards climb up walls! This little Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) on our house wasn't moving much today, and he let me get close to photograph his rear-left foot clinging to the cracks in the wood. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2004)

I'll bet there's a good ergonomic reason for the odd spacing of the toes on this rear foot, heading to the right. Think of track sprinters and how *fast* these lizards are...

File.Name: 4c20d_4c19-113.jpg
Notes: 9087368 This Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus fultoni or kin) was perched on the side of our house where the sun never quite shines on the north-facing slope of the mountains. It's probably still there. This is the classic "thermometer cricket" - count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 to get the temperature. As we approach the winter solstice, the crickets are silent, with treefrogs starting to take their place in the evening chorus jam. I was surprised to find this cricket today - do they overwinter as adults? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c20e_4c19-120.jpg
Notes: 9087264 Tiny but gnarly spider in the garden. I reckon this spider is a "Wolf Spider" (Lycosidae or kin) since it doesn't have a web. The flower is a Sage (Salvia) in our garden - maybe S. blepharophylla from Mexico? - that is still covered with flowers despite hard frost and not much sunlight. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c20f_4c19-123.jpg
Notes: 9087102 First flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in our canyon. They remind me of beautiful exotic dancers - red skirts/sepals held high in the air, white petticoats/petals underneath, and arrowhead-shaped "sagittate" stamens and demure pistils exposed to the world. I'm not exaggerating - that's what flowers are for! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c21h_4c21-150.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10292313 Cortinarius mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees. The mushrooms have a cobwebby "cortina" veil that covers the immature gills and catches the orange-brown falling spores as the cap opens. Probably not edible but still beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c21a_4c21-142.jpg
Notes:
9094716 This is real - it's part of the same cluster of Slime Molds with stalked sporangia from the [Previous] photo growing on a piece of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) bark that I moved into the sunlight for this photo. They look like little chickies framed by those out-of-focus leaves in the background! They are really stalked fungus-like sporangia that will release spores to carry the single-celled amoeba-like protists to a "new log in the shade". The closer I look, the more interesting nature becomes! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

Fotolog has been cranky tonight, and so has my computer. Don't install the new AVG 7.0 virus scanner upgrade - something's not right!

File.Name: 4c21b_4c21-141.jpg
Notes: 9094679 Slime Mold with stalked sporangia growing on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia)log and bark. You have to look very close to find these remarkable critters that are part animal and part fungus. Single celled amoeba-like protists live in decaying wood, but they get an "urge to merge" and stream together dissolving their cell-walls to form a multinucleate mass of protoplasm called a "plasmodium" that dries to become this mushroom-like structure to release spores that will become more amoeba-like protists. It's a perfect strategy for single-celled organisms to move the relatively huge distance from one decaying log to another. I took this photo in the shade, then moved a piece into the sun for the [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c21c_4c21-153.jpg
Notes: 9094481 Tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) among the fallen leaves and brambles. They really are tiny. For scale, look at that spine on the oak leaf below the left cap. I don't know why the micro-world is so pretty - but it is! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c21d_4c21-169.jpg
Notes: 9094424 Entoloma rhodopolium mushrooms in the woods. The upper cap is making a natural spore print on the lower showing "pink" or flesh colored spores. This is a big satisfying mushroom that you can grab with your hand, but don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c21e_4c21-163.jpg
Notes:
9094410 Yes, we have no bananas! They look like bananas, but these are male pollen-bearing stamens on dangling staminate catkins of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Our native plants always flower at different times depending on the year, but the sequence is usually tight. This oak tree by our mail box is a couple of months early. Every year is different. This year we had early rain, then cold, now warm. Ntive and garden plants are confused, and so am I. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

I have a photo in the archives of the same oak tree flowering in March last year, with wasp galls on the catkins. See:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7616543

Now it's flowering three months early with NO insect galls. I wonder what the gall wasps will do in a few months?

Is this just another interesting year, or is it one of those "global warming" things? People are asking that question around the world!

9094273 You know those tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams - Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats and lots more. When I first saw the double trails, I figured I was jittery from too much coffee. Looking closer, I think I understand. The tiny flies reflect sunlight from each wing with every wing beat. Depending on their path, they make double trails or those fanastic "snake-vertebrae" trails like little moving strobe lights. Knowing shutter speed and lens magnification, I could learn a lot from these photos - like how fast they move and beat their wings, and maybe what the heck they are up to. File.Name: 4c21g_4c21-143.jpg
Notes: 9104119 Pretty LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - hiding in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. For every little 'shroom I see, I must walk over (or on) hundreds more. The stumper is that some mushrooms hide, while others stand out with colors as bright as the most brilliant wildflowers! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c25a_4c23-216.jpg
Notes:
9104184 Fotolog's still being cranky tonight, but I'm not. This is a brilliant Sage (Salvia) in our garden - maybe S. blepharophylla from Mexico? It's still flowering despite hard frost and not much sunlight. I hope we are all so brilliant on this dark holiday night, and looking beyond! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2004)

I love our community here. I wish you all the best for the holidays and the new year!

File.Name: 4c27i_4c23-202.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 9364922 I wonder if this Dragonfly (Anisoptera), maybe a female Darner (Aeshnidae?) will perch here until spring? (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27h_4c18-018.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10330230 Another of those pretty LBMs - "Little Brown Mushroom" - growing under the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27g_4c21-158.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10330174 Pretty little mushrooms growing from oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27a_4c23-201.jpg
Notes:
9113558 I was surprised to find this large Dragonfly (Anisoptera), maybe a female Darner (Aeshnidae?) hanging from a twig in the canyon not far from the mostly dry creek bed. I haven't seen a dragonfly in the air for weeks. I poked it - maybe it twiched, but it didn't budge. I wonder if it will perch here until spring? It's a real stumper how any and all seasonal critters manage to survive from one year to the next! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

Welcome back Fotolog - I wish you a great new year!

File.Name: 4c27b_4c23-192.jpg
Notes: 9113407 Strange view inside the head of this large Dragonfly (Anisoptera), maybe a female Darner (Aeshnidae?), that I found perched in the canyon. (See [Next] photo.) Those huge eyes aren't enough - they can also swivel their heads around this ball-and-socket neck joint. I guess that's why it's so hard to sneak up on these critters. It's interesting that among mammals and birds, the prey species usually have eyes set far apart for wide peripheral vision to see danger coming from behind, while preditors have eyes close together for better focus and depth perception. It seems that dragonflies are different. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27c_4c23-185.jpg
Notes: 9113185 Elegant new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) catching a beam of sunlight in the winter-dark woods - like a beautiful dancer "en pointe" with skirts and petticoats aflutter. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27d_4c23-189.jpg
Notes: 9113120 This deciduous Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) shrub is already putting out new leaves. Usually they leaf out in spring, but we just passed the winter solsice. Every year is different, so I'm not surprised that native plants get confused. I think of it like a gamble that sometimes pays off. Last week was sunny and warm. Today it's cold and stormy. I can almost hear that native plant muttering "D'oh!" (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27e_4c23-210.jpg
Notes: 9113020 Jaws! No, this isn't a monster from an RPG game. It's a mushroom in the woods. Some critter munched into the cap and opened a hole before the mushroom was ready to release spores. I guess it's a Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea) since it is part of a cluster growing from an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump. These caps have a "partial veil" covering the gills that will fall away to become a ring on the stems. The veil hasn't opened yet, but you can look through the critter-opening to see what the partial veil looks like from the inside. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c27f_4c21-157.jpg
Notes:
9112955 Tender shot of Common Psathyrella (Psathyrella condolleana or kin) mushrooms in the canyon under the oaks. They are small LBMs - "Little Brown Mushroom" - decomposers with dark spores. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2004)

Sure it's been hard to upload for a few days, but hope is appropriate for the solstice and the holidays, so I'll try again. I've had some extra time with my kids and my parents, the sun is a bit higher in the sky (just a bit) than it was yesterday, and a good storm is dropping real rain. Bring it on!
Locality: just a bit) than it was yesterday

File.Name: 4c28g_4c18-037.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10348668 Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) on a dead oak log in the canyon. It looks like a typical bracket or polypore fungus from above, but it has real gills below - see [Previous] photo. Notice the unusual green algae bands on the cap. I wonder if this critter is part lichen as well as part polypore and part agaric? Did all lichens start this way? (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c28h_4c18-054.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10348530 Looking at "gills" under the cap of this Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) on a dead oak log. It looks like a typical bracket or polypore fungus from above, but it has real gills below. Look closer towards the bottom to see how some of the gills become tubes! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c28a_4c28-030.jpg
Notes: 9123599 "Oh the wind and the rain" - this Fuschia flower in a pot by the house is collecting raindrops from the storm. We've had 7+ inches of rain since yesterday with more coming. This will keep the woods and creeks alive for next year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c28b_4c28-024.jpg
Notes:
9123450 A thin film of rain water is connecting these Common Bedstraw Galium aparine) seedlings. Across a half-inch of soil or the hemisphere of our planet, we can join hands (or cotyledons) in support of tsunami victims. As Phil Ochs sang, "There but for fortune, may go you or go I." (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2004)

One place to help is Direct Relief International at:
   http://www.directrelief.org/

File.Name: 4c28c_4c28-025.jpg
Notes: 9123267 Our storm performed a magical balancing act to arrange these perfect raindrops on seedling leaflets of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius). Look close - you can see the woods reflected! How can such beautiful order come from the chaos of a storm? (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c28d_4c27-015.jpg
Notes: 9123155 "Oh the wind and the rain" - it's not dreadful at all! We've had 7 inches of rain so far from this storm, and more is coming to keep the woods and creeks alive for next year. This is looking at a sapling Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree from under the porch. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c28e_4c26-009.jpg
Notes: 9123059 Ferns, bulbs, and Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) leaves in my parents' garden. (Santa Monica Mountains, 26 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c28f_4c25-002.jpg
Notes: 9123009 Ma and Pa Treebeard? Nah, but I`m proud we made all our holiday gifts this year instead of being consumers. Julie made the apple dolls and I whittled the fish from a scrap of pine board that twisted just right. We gave this to my Mom and Dad. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29h_4c29-066.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10363908 Looking close at a Boletus truncatus(?) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It has brilliant topological detail - I see canyons and hanging creeks to explore! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29g_4c29-050.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10363849 Slimy cap of Cortinarius varius or kin - It has a violet-tinged stem without a bulbous base. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29a_4c29-066.jpg
Notes: 9131767 I guess this is a Boletus truncatus(?) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The cap is soggy, but it has brilliant topological detail - I see canyons and hanging creeks to explore! We`ve had 8+ inches of rain over the past three days, no problems. We associate mushrooms and rain, but the `shrooms somehow know they can`t disperse spores in the rain so they wait a few days. I found today`s mushrooms after the rain stopped this afternoon, but they are all old caps that were already formed and waiting in the oak mulch. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29b_4c29-059.jpg
Notes: 9131637 I'm sure this is a Russula mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). We've had 8+ inches of rain this week, but this tattered 'shroom must have come up during the dry spell before. Look how the cap is split open and eaten away inside. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29c_4c29-047.jpg
Notes: 9131580 New Milky Cap mushroom, probably Lactarius chrysorheus or kin, though I didn't pick it to make sure. (Scratch the gills - do they exude a white latex that soon turns yellow?) The grass is probably native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29d_4c29-053.jpg
Notes: 9131500 Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch. Most of those spots are tiny Springtail (Collembia) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Springtail

File.Name: 4c29e_4c29-051.jpg
Notes: 9131467 I ruined the party by turning this mushroom over - what a mass of tiny Springtail (Collembia) insects are hiding here! I believe the mushroom is Cortinarius varius or kin - large slimy cap and violet-tinged stem without a bulbous base. This mushroom might be edible, or not - but it's a good lesson to shake out every 'shroom before eating - many of them are inhabited! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c29f_4c29-057.jpg
Notes: 9131377 One of those fungi that grows on dead wood and really isn't a "mushroom" at all. The little caps are lined up across this fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twig like an amusement park ride! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c30a_4c27-017.jpg
Notes:
9138558 Another rainy day on the mountain. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2004)

It's been dark and drizzly all day. I never went past the porch, but it's a perfect day for my annual New Years backup ritual to burn everything important to CD (again), delete all that stuff I haven't looked at all year, and defrag the whole mess. You should do the same!

File.Name: 4c30b_4c29-064.jpg
Notes:
9138471 I've lived in these mountains for 30+ years, and I'm still often stumped by what I find in the woods. This looks like a "loogie" (spelling?) on the rock with water dripping down through mosses and ferns and liverworts. This foam is pretty solid, look close and see a few tiny Springtail (Collembia) insects on the surface. There's also some foam in the creek. It's a stumper - are these suds natural or a sign of pollution? (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2004)

My guess is the foam is natural - the run-off is a thick soup of proteins and spores that's rich enough to attract the springtails to feed. I hope there's no pollution up here in the mountains. This is my home at the top of the watershed!

File.Name: 4c31a_4c31-089.jpg
Notes: 9143834 We've had 15+ inches of rain this week - and enough to fill a coffee can last night! There's water in the creek for the first time in a few years! Creeks flow and time passes - I reckon it's a good image for New Year's Eve. I took this photo at f/25 @ 1/15 @ ISO 200 to show the motion. I'm old enough to think about the flow of time as well as water, and doing what matters. Environmental issues need our attention this new year before we lose too much that can't be replaced. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2004)

They look like another "LBM" ("Little Brown Mushroom"), but the stalks are polished and brittle and feel smooth like wire. The caps do NOT have the beautiful frosted look of Marasmius plicatulus. associatedTaxa: Marasmius fuscopurpureus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius plicatulus

File.Name: 4c31e_4c31-115.jpg
Notes:
9143745 This is one of those "Nature Who Am I" photos...

It's a pencil-point sized Mite (Acari) - a very tiny spider relative - hitching a ride on the back of a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin). Think of Whale Rider and Dune! Looking close, it seems this mite only has 6 legs. I think that means it's immature and is still a molt or two away from being an adult. I also noticed a bright red mite nearby, but it wouldn't stand still for a photo. Maybe that's the adult? I have another photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8759089

Nature is rich and deep and full of surprises. Who would ever expect to find "Banana Slug Mites"? Choose your obsessions well, and worry that the world is becoming simpler because of bad choices. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c31f_4c31-071.jpg
Notes: 9143714 We've had 15+ inches of rain this week - and enough to fill a coffee can last night! Here's where some of it goes. This *sometimes* waterfall is in the canyon east of the "Old San Marcos Road" on the west fork of Maria Ygnacio Creek. For scale look at that mature oak tree to the right of the upper falls. The cascades keep going down enough further for another photo. I call it "Heart Falls" because of the shape of the rocks. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2004)

This is my last photo for a few days. Julie and I are heading out to Santa Cruz Island to kill weeds for the Nature Conservancy. Of course I`m taking my camera!

File.Name: 3710b_3709-153.jpg
Notes: The flowers of Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) aren`t much, but the typical scorpioid inflorescence - curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle neck - is very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2003)

File.Name: 3710c_3710-171.jpg
Notes: Tree Tobacco (Nicotiana glauca) is a common weedy shrub introduced into California from South America back in Spanish days. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 July 2003)

File.Name: 3710d_3710-166.jpg
Notes: Castor Bean (Ricinus communis) is a weedy shrub in the Santa Barbara foothills. I`m glad it doesn`t survive up in the mountains where I live. It has strange flowers and toxic beans that contain ricin, one of the most poisonous naturally occuring substances known. It`s also the source of castor bean oil which is a laxative and the best soak to condition rubber bands for model airplanes. My flora says it was cultivated in SB during WW1 for airplane oil. Try a web search on these keywords. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 July 2003)

File.Name: 3710e_3710-162.jpg
Notes: Bristly Ox Tongue (Picris echioides) is a nasty weed that is spreading in Santa Barbara County. But it has a dreamy beauty when over-exposed and over-saturated like this. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 July 2003)
