File.Name: 3716b_3713-091.jpg
Notes: Not flowers but insect galls on endemic Santa Cruz Island Manzanita (Arctostaphylos insularis). These pretty galls are caused by aphids, and you can carefully uncurl an infected leaf and see them inside. All local manzanitas seem to get these galls. (Santa Cruz Island, 13 July 2003)

File.Name: 3716c_3713-141.jpg
Notes: It looks like a sea anemone under water, but it`s Crystalline Iceplant (Mesembryanthemum crystallinum). I guess that`s why they call it ICEplant. Surreal crystals form on the leaves and buds. *Sigh* It`s another invasive weed that takes over native beach habitats. (Santa Cruz Island, 13 July 2003)

File.Name: 3716d_3713-113.jpg
Notes: Pine cones of Santa Cruz Island Pine (Pinus muricata forma remorata). It`s really just a form of Bishop Pine (Pinus muricata) that has +/- symmetrical cones that stand out from the stem. The trees I saw were variable, as are the native Bishop Pines at Lompoc and Orcutt. Pretty cool to be in a pine forest on the island! (Santa Cruz Island, 13 July 2003)
CommonNames: Santa Cruz Island Pine; Bishop Pine

File.Name: 3716e_3714-180.jpg
Notes: Insect galls on endemic Island Ceanothus (Ceanothus arboreus), presumably caused by a Cynipidae gall wasp. These interesting galls were large, over an inch across. (Santa Cruz Island, 13 July 2003)

File.Name: 3716f_3713-066.jpg
Notes: Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) is one of the invasive weeds that we targeted in our restoration work at Santa Cruz Island last week. Pretty flowers and tasty roots and seeds, but look at that wall of plants in the background. Fennel has taken over thousands of acres on the Channel Islands after the sheep and cattle were eliminated. What a mess. (Santa Cruz Island, 13 July 2003)

File.Name: 3716h_3714-189.jpg
Notes: Snug as a bug in a ... Island Buckwheat (Eriogonum grande var. grande). I don`t know who or what made the hole, but it looks comfy. (Santa Cruz Island, 14 July 2003)

File.Name: 3716k_2222-018.jpg
Notes: Ring around the moon. Look close and there`s a hint of color. It really is a rainbow! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2002)

File.Name: 3717a_3715-288.jpg
Notes: Fruit of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) with dramatic natural lighting. The ripe berries are tasty if you can figure out how to get in. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2003)

File.Name: 3717b_3715-322.jpg
Notes: Tiny flowers of California Figwort (Scrophularia californica). (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2003)

File.Name: 3717c_3715-319.jpg
Notes: I think this introduced grass is Red Brome (Bromus madritensis ssp. rubens). It`s an invading weed along our road, but it`s pretty with backlighting. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2003)

File.Name: 3717d_3716-333.jpg
Notes: Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) is having a good year for making seeds. They do well in pots if you want an unusual house plant. Just don`t touch! (Santa Ynez River, 16 July 2003)

File.Name: 3717e_3715-281.jpg
Notes: Looking up at a dreamy mass of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). There`s another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2003)

File.Name: 3717f_3715-324.jpg
Notes: The morning after... There`s something forlorn about native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) flowers that only last one night. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2003)

File.Name: 3717g_3715-325.jpg
Notes:
Sometimes I just like to sit and dream...

I think this critter is a

Pacific Chorus Frog (Pseudacris regilla) aka Pacific Tree Frog (Hyla regilla). (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718a_3717-396.jpg
Notes: Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii) is one of our most amazing native wildflowers. It just started flowering. It`s not common. You have to hunt for it in dry exposed rocky places, often hiding under other plants. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718b_3717-382.jpg
Notes: Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) flowers all through our dry summer. This looks like a miniature parabolic solar collector aimed at the green stigma. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718c_3717-431.jpg
Notes: Peeling bark on Madrone (Arbutus menziesii), related to manzanita. It feels so good to rub! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718d_3717-410.jpg
Notes: California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium aka Zauschneria californica) is a classic summer hummingbird flower. These are the first blooms I`ve seen this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718e_3717-350.jpg
Notes: Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum). It`s remarkable that it`s here near Sata Barbara. The birds usually beat me to the berries. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718f_3717-370.jpg
Notes: Oleander (Nerium oleander) is such a common landscaping shrub that I rarely notice it. It`s seriously toxic too. But walking along my neighbor`s fence, they were catching the light just so, beautiful in a Georgia O`Keeffe kind of way. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)

File.Name: 3718g_3717-440.jpg
Notes: One of those moments... I was catching my breath from a hard hike when a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly touched down on the Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) I was about to photograph. I swung the camera around for a quickie... (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2003)
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus rutulus; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus rutulus; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum

File.Name: 3719a_3718-449.jpg
Notes: Detail of Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii). It`s not a weed! These amazing summer flowers are irresistible. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2003)

File.Name: 3719b_3718-446.jpg
Notes: Detail of Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii). (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2003)

File.Name: 3719c_3718-456.jpg
Notes: Detail of Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii). (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2003)

File.Name: 3719d_3718-447.jpg
Notes: Detail of Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii). (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2003)

File.Name: 3719e_3718-459.jpg
Notes: Detail of a spent Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii), one of our most remarkable summer wildflowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2003)

File.Name: 3719f_3718-465.jpg
Notes: Gray Pine aka Digger Pine (Pinus sabiniana) in the clouds. We actually got a welcome bit of summer rain from the remnant of Hurricane Claudette. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2003)

File.Name: 3719h_3716-345.jpg
Notes: I think this is an antlion pit (Myrmeleon). Think about the movies Invaders From Mars and Tremors, the antlion larvae drag their victems down! There`s info here. I tried whispering "Doodle bug, doodle bug, come out of your hole", but he didn`t. (Santa Ynez River, 16 July 2003)

File.Name: NA
Notes: NA

File.Name: 3720a_3719-039.jpg
Notes: Coyote Mint (Monardella hypoleuca) is a shrubby mint relative with a strong smell that`s not quite pleasant. I have a photo of another native monardella here. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3720b_3719-028.jpg
Notes: Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) is a common native subshrub in the shady woods around my house, almost a weed. For some reason, my Olympus digital camera always has a hard time with that particular shade of yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3720c_3719-030.jpg
Notes: Prickley Lettuce (Lactuca serriola) is a common weed, but the young leaf veins are so beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3720d_3719-032.jpg
Notes: Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) has pretty little rose flowers now, but they are terrible to bushwack through when they finish and turn brown and crumbly. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3720e_3719-025.jpg
Notes: Nice geometry in native grass leaves. I think it`s a pubescent form of Giant Rye (Leymus condensatus), but nothing is flowering yet so I`m not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3720f_3719-021.jpg
Notes: Odd flowers of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum). They have a sweet maple syrup smell, and they make nice arrangements when dry, but they always end up dusty and full of spider webs. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3720g_3719-006.jpg
Notes: Ant on Milkweed flower in our garden. Think how BIG the garden is for that little critter! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721a_3719-036.jpg
Notes: Remarkable flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum). I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721b_3720-098.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). Look close in the empty calyx facing out just to the right of the flower and you can see the four new seeds sliced like a pie. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721c_3719-051.jpg
Notes: The ripening seed heads of White Sage (Salvia apiana) show beautiful colors after flowering. I have a photo of the white flowers here. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721d_3720-115.jpg
Notes: Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis? but cp. T. nodosa) - otherwise known as "those $#@%! sticky seeds that ruined my socks!" The seeds are like tiny cockleburs, a terrible pest for socks and dogs. Don`t pick out the seeds in the woods! We`re waging war against this new weed as it tries to invade our canyon. Be sure to bag everything you pick! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721e_3720-099.jpg
Notes: Common Madia or Tarweed (Madia elegans ssp. densifolia) has sticky glandular flowers and foliage. The pretty composite flowers open at night and dawn but close during the day. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721f_3720-084.jpg
Notes: Smooth Scouring Rush (aka Horsetail) (Equisetum hyemale ssp. affine). This is a lush little pond that catches the overflow from our hot/cold tub. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2003)

File.Name: 3721h_3720-106.jpg
Notes: This tattered Buckeye (Precis coenia) was caught in a spider web. I don`t know if that other photographer turned over the tortoise, but this butterfly flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722a_3721-158.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly on the clown-colored milkweed in our garden. (Series 6 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722b_3721-128.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly on the clown-colored milkweed in our garden. (Series 5 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722c_3721-164.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly in motion on the clown-colored milkweed in our garden. (Series 4 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722d_3721-161.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly in motion on the clown-colored milkweed in our garden. (Series 3 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722e_3721-148.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly on the clown-colored milkweed in our garden. (Series 2 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722f_3721-127.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly on the clown-colored milkweed in our garden. (Series 1 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3722g_3721-133.jpg
Notes: Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis orpifex?) on a salvia in our garden. These bees have a trick. They burrow into the calyx at the base of the flower to get the nectar without pollinating the flower. The large bees drill neat 3/4 inch holes in the fascia boards of our house to make their nests. They have my blessing. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2003)

File.Name: 3723a_3722-192.jpg
Notes: This Crawdad chased away a small fish and then tried to chase ME away as I took pictures from the shore! He`s in about 4 inches of water. I put my digital camera on manual focus so the auto-focus wouldn`t be tricked by surface reflections, and I used PSP to "stretch the histogram" and bump the contrast a bit. There`s another photo in the Bugs N` Critters group. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)

File.Name: 3723b_3722-209.jpg
Notes: Seed heads on willow (Salix). (Willow species are very hard to tell apart!) The tiny seeds are hidden in the cottony fluff and blow in the wind like dandelions. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)

File.Name: 3723c_3722-179.jpg
Notes: Insect galls on the leaves of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii. I believe these common galls are caused by the Poplar Petiole Gall Aphid (Pemphigus populitransversus). (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)
CommonNames: Fremont Cottonwood; Poplar Petiole Gall Aphid

File.Name: 3723d_3722-188.jpg
Notes: Cattail (Typha angustifolia?). The seed heads are fun to play with, but be careful with your camera! I got a great photo once of kids stripping the seed heads. Some of the fluff got into my camera lens mechanism, and it still grinds when the lens extends. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)

File.Name: 3723e_3722-201.jpg
Notes: Cattails (Typha angustifolia?) on the edge of our secret swimming hole. It`s not deep, but the water is still flowing. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)

File.Name: 3723f_3722-172.jpg
Notes: What can kill a mighty oak? This Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) on the way to our swimming hole completely died since last year. Valley Oaks (Q. lobata) can stand dead for years, but Live Oaks literally fall apart until nothing is left. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)

File.Name: 3723g_3722-195.jpg
Notes: A little nature drama at our swimming hole! The fish tried to nip the crawdad who stood him off. Then the aggressive crawdad came right at me! This is in about 6 inches of (not very clear) water. There`s another photo at my fotolog. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724a_3723-277.jpg
Notes: Developing seeds of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum), which are finishing up now in our canyon. May they prosper and multiply! I have photos of the flowers here and here. The out-of-focus tree in the background is Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa). (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724b_3723-294.jpg
Notes: Seed pods of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) twist up to squeeze out the seeds. The last seed is about to drop. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724c_3723-284.jpg
Notes: New fruit on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis). (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724d_3723-280.jpg
Notes: These should be developing fruit on our Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus), but I don`t see many berries this year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724e_3723-248.jpg
Notes: The leaves of Shiny-leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) have beautiful geometry, like 3D surface graphs. They are not common here. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724f_3723-258.jpg
Notes: Red air roots on Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). This is how the vines climb up into the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3724g_3723-251.jpg
Notes: Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae) hanging under it`s web. They are everywhere in our woods now. The webs look messy, but they are effective. Check this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3725a_3724-384.jpg
Notes: This is another one of those magic moments that only happen because of fotolog. I`ve been hiking and looking in the woods every day to get new photos for this page. Yesterday I found a tiny adolescent Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna) getting lessons from Mom on how to hover and feed from flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). Here he gets everything right! Look close at the open wing and see how the veins are all double for strength. The little hummer had absolutely no concern for me, and even took a nap on the ground at my feet. Mom chit-chit-chitted, but she put up with me. I feel blessed to share the moment. (I have another baby bird encounter here.) (Series 6 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)
ScientificName: every day; Calypta anna; Keckiella cordifolia; chit-chit-chitted
associatedTaxa: every day; Calypta anna; Keckiella cordifolia; chit-chit-chitted

File.Name: 3725b_3724-366.jpg
Notes: The little adolescent Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna) gets it right hovering and feeding from a Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) flower. Look close and you can see how pollination happens. The yellow stamens of the flower are visible rubbing the top of the little hummer`s head with pollen that he will carry to the next flower. Mom must be proud. (Series 5 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3725c_3724-370.jpg
Notes: Tiny adolescent Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna) learning how to feed from Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) flowers. He`s hanging onto the flower with those tiny feet instead of hovering. Look close at the open wing and see how the veins are all double for strength. (Series 4 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3725d_3724-357.jpg
Notes: Mom stayed close by teaching her baby Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna) how to feed in the Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). Reminds me of teaching my kids to drive. (Series 3 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3725e_3724-360.jpg
Notes: Tiny adolescent Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna) stretching after a nap in Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). (Series 2 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3725f_3724-379.jpg
Notes: Nap time for this tiny adolescent Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna). He napped for almost five minutes, perched on a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). He took several more naps while feeding, once on the ground at my feet. Mom disappeared for a while, so I could get close. He`s about the size of an apricot. (Series 1 of 6, San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3725g_3724-379.jpg
Notes: Momma keeping an eye on her baby Anna`s Hummingbird (Calypta anna) learning how to feed in the Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). Reminds me of teaching my kids to drive. He`s about the size of an apricot. I have more photos and the story at Treebeard`s Photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726a_3724-308.jpg
Notes: Flower Fly (Syrphidae) on California Wild Rose (Rosa californica). These pretty flies mimic bees, but they move much faster. Note how the wings are almost invisible. I was lucky this time. This critter flew into my photo just as I pushed the button. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726b_3725-422.jpg
Notes: Tent Caterpillars (Lasiocampidae) enshrouding a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). They favor local penstemon for some reason and sometimes wrap up whole plants like a 50`s sci-fi movie. They give me the creeps. Usually the caterpillars live inside the tent, but these were crawling around outside. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726c_3724-334.jpg
Notes: The feet of Water Striders (Gerridae) have special water-repellent hairs that keep them afloat and make those cool shadows. I took this photo in a very dark shaded pool on our mostly dry creek with my flash turned a few stops down. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726d_3723-265.jpg
Notes: The web of a Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae). They are everywhere in our woods now. The webs look messy, but they are effective. Check these photos here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726e_3724-324.jpg
Notes: Scavenger Fly (Sarcophagidae) on a fox or coyote scat that`s mostly grass and Manzanita berries. These noisy flies look like overgrown house flies with a red tipped butt. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726f_3724-388.jpg
Notes: This Sphinx Moth (Sphingidae) was napping on an oak tree. He vibrated his wings for a while and took off from my hand. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2003)

File.Name: 3726g_3725-427.jpg
Notes: Tent Caterpillars (Lasiocampidae) enshrouding a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). They give me the creeps. I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2003)

File.Name: 3727a_3725-413.jpg
Notes:
Photographers and hummingbirds can`t resist the summer flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). Late afternoon light helps. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2003

I`m off to Oregon tomorrow for a week or two on the North Umpqua River. I`m thinking of summer Steelhead. I`m sure I`ll return with lots of new photos!

File.Name: 3727b_3724-299.jpg
Notes: Elegant tendrils of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus). I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3727c_3724-317.jpg
Notes: Flowers on a tiny native Bedstraw (maybe Galium californicum ssp. flaccidum?). Those oak leaves are only about an inch long. We have many native bedstraw species and I find them almost impossible to key out. I reckon this is evolution in action. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3727d_3724-314.jpg
Notes: Is this a beautiful infection that turns some leaves red on our Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis)? I think it might be caused by the special aphids that make leaf galls on these plants. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3727e_3725-404.jpg
Notes: Prickly Sow-Thistle (Sonchus asper) is a common weed that`s not on anyone`s list of favorite plants, but it has its own beauty with late afternoon backlight. I bagged it before those seeds could spread. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2003)

File.Name: 3727f_3724-346.jpg
Notes: The birds have already eaten most of the fruit on our native Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana). The berries have a beautiful white "glaucous" coating that soon rubs away. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3727h_3724-326.jpg
Notes: Scavenger Fly (Sarcophagidae) on a fox or coyote scat that`s mostly undigested grass and Manzanita berries. These noisy flies look like overgrown house flies with a red tipped butt. I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806a_3731-083.jpg
Notes: Sporangia of Western Sword Fern (Polystichum munitum) in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806b_3730-036.jpg
Notes: Wasp galls (from the gall wasp Cynips maculipennis?) on leaves of Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana). Galls are fascinating tumor-like plant growths caused by specialized insects and other organisms. There`s some info at Wayne`s Word. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806c_3730-011.jpg
Notes: Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae) hanging under it`s web. These spiders build a scaffold to pull their web up into a dome shape. The beautiful scintillating colors must be the result of refraction, like the surface of a CD. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806d_3730-009.jpg
Notes: Rattlesnake Orchid (Goodyera oblongifolia) still blooming despite the heat. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806e_3731-115.jpg
Notes: Developing fruit of Red Huckleberry (Vaccinium parvifolium). The berries are edible, but they don`t have much taste. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806f_3731-104.jpg
Notes: It`s a common weed in Oregon, but the flower heads of Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota) still hold a secret. In the very center there is usually a single red-purple flower! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3806h_3729-001.jpg
Notes: Spider Wasp (Pompillidae) on Sulphur Buckwheat (Eriogonum umbellatum). (Klamath River, California, 29 July 2003)

File.Name: 3807a_3731-144.jpg
Notes: Beautiful peeling bark on Madrone (Arbutus menziesii). I have another photo here. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3807b_3731-132.jpg
Notes: The ripe fruit of Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) are edible, but not very substantial. I usually look for flowers caught in a beam of afternoon sunlight against a dark background. But I like this shade-loving plant caught with this bright background. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3807c_3801-182.jpg
Notes: Ripening fruit on Salal (Gaultheria shallon). The berries are edible but bland. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 August 2003)

File.Name: 3807d_3731-085.jpg
Notes: Fritillary butterfly, maybe Leto Fritillary (Speyeria cybele leto?) resting on Hazelnut (Corylus cornuta) leaf. She was very active. This was a lucky shot. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3807e_3730-023.jpg
Notes: Bog Wintergreen (Pyrola asarifolia) developing seeds after flowering. It`s an uncommon woodland herb that always makes me think about elephants! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2003)

File.Name: 3807f_3731-109.jpg
Notes: Tiny flowers on Trail Plant (Adenocaulon bicolor) with nice natural lighting. This woodland plant has handsome large leaves, but the tiny composite flowers look like nothing. They are a challenge to photograph. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3807i_3731-080.jpg
Notes: Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae) visiting Wild Teasel (Dipsacus fullonum). (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3808a_3802-241.jpg
Notes: Ox-eye Daisy (Leucanthemum vulgare) is an invasive weed that we found everywhere in Oregon. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3808b_3802-260.jpg
Notes: Looking into a seed head of Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota). The seeds look like little critters with snouts who are also looking in! Those prickery seeds can destroy socks and sandals! I have a photo of the flowers here. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3808c_3802-207.jpg
Notes: Purple Loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria) has pretty flowers, but it`s an invasive weed that can take over wetlands like this bank on the Willamette River. There`s info at CalEPPC. Did you notice the small wild bee at the top of the photo? (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3808d_3802-234.jpg
Notes: Opening seed pod of Willow-Herb (Epilobium ciliatum?) on the bank of the Willamette River. The dandelion-like seeds form in the extended ovary below the small flowers. Here the seed pod is splitting and the seeds are making their way into the world. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3808e_3802-243.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Moth Mullein (Verbascum blattaria) on the bank of the Willamette River. I like the flowers but it`s a weed that`s starting to spread along roads in the Santa Barbara mountains at home. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3808f_3802-224.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Creek Clematis (Clematis ligusticifolia) on the bank of the Willamette River. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3808h_3808-403.jpg
Notes: Female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly visiting a Dahlia in our garden. There was also a male fluttering by. The males (but not the famales) have an amazing irridescent "dog face" pattern on their upper wings, but they hide it as soon as they land and fold their wings up. It`s our California State Butterfly for good reason, but it looks like another Sulphur unless you get the right view as it flutters by. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809a_3804-322.jpg
Notes: New leaves on a sapling Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum). (Upper Sacramento River, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809b_3804-391.jpg
Notes: Pretty mystery thistle along a creek behind our motel in Weed, California. There were other suspicious plants nearby, so this may have come from a "wildflower mix" seed packet. The stems and large leaves are not prickery. Please leave a note if you recognize it. (Upper Sacramento River, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809c_3804-294.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Bouncing Bet aka Soapwort (Saponaria officinalis). It`s related to Carnations and Pinks (Caryophyllaceae). Bouncing Bet has pretty phlox-like flowers and I love the name, but it`s another invasive weed that takes over wetlands and stream banks. (Upper Sacramento River, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809d_3804-330.jpg
Notes: The brilliant unripe berries of Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) look inviting, but they are a powerful cathartic laxative. (Upper Sacramento River, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809e_3804-346.jpg
Notes: It`s ironic that the common wild blackberry throught the NorthWest is Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor), introduced from Europe despite the name. Common and tasty! (Upper Sacramento River, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809f_3804-310.jpg
Notes: The Sims Creek Bridge is one of my favorites. It`s a small but structurally perfect 160 foot suspension bridge across the Sacramento River above Lake Shasta. A plaque tells the story that it was built in 1933 and was the first major construction project built by any CCC program in the US. The crew figured it out as they went along without regulations. The bridge is still in good shape, notice the camber. I wish I could take my middle school science and shop classes to visit! (Upper Sacramento River, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3809h_3809-004.jpg
Notes: Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) in our garden feeding on Dahlia. Note the extended proboscis sucking up the good stuff. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2003)

File.Name: 3810a_3805-370.jpg
Notes: Looking into a Common Sunflower (Helianthus annuus) growing along the road. This is a small-flowered wild relative of the garden plant. Finally a native "weed" in a disturbed habitat that belongs here! (Highway 33 near Coalinga, 5 August 2003)

File.Name: 3810b_3731-133.jpg
Notes: I`ve been calling this pretty weed Spotted Kapweed (Centaurea maculosa), but now I think it might be the hybrid Meadow Thistle (Centaurea xpratensis). (See here and here.) Is this evolution in action? It`s still a weed, and it`s still very pretty, and the local butterflies still love it. This photo focuses on the remarkable fringed brown phylaries that hold the base of the flower head like a many-handed fan dancer. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3810c_3802-201.jpg
Notes: Tansy (Tanacetum vulgare) is another introduced weed with some traditional herbal uses, but it can be toxic in large doses. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)

File.Name: 3810d_3802-228.jpg
Notes: This Centaury (Centaurium erythraea) plant is a Gentian relative. It is common in the Oregon woods, and I always assumed it`s native because it looks so much like our native Canchalagua (C. venustum and C. davyi) back home near Santa Barbara. Nope, it`s another introduced weed native to Europe. At least it`s pretty, and it doesn`t seem too nasty in displacing the natives. (Portland, Oregon, 2 August 2003)
ScientificName: Centaurium erythraea; Gentian; C. venustum; C. davyi
associatedTaxa: Centaurium erythraea; Gentian; C. venustum; C. davyi

File.Name: 3810e_3804-285.jpg
Notes: Flowers on Wild Teasel (Dipsacus fullonum). It`s another beautiful weed. I have a closer photo showing the geometry in the texture group. (Portland, Oregon, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3810f_3731-067.jpg
Notes: A grove of small Oregon Ash (Fraxinus latifolia) trees in the Oregon woods. This place is usually boggy and the trees are still shaggy with moss, but it was crunchy-dry this summer. There are no reflections here, just shadows with the sun straight behind. I know it`s a little fuzzy, but I still love this photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3810h_3810-052.jpg
Notes: Is it a bumblebee? Is it a hummingbird? No! I`m pretty sure this is a Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis), also called a Bumblebee or Hummingbird Moth for good reasons. It`s a strange kind of Sphinx Moth (Sphingidae) that mimics a large bumblebee (about an inch long) and hovers like a hummingbird to suck nectar from flowers. This beauty breaks all the usual moth rules with clear wings and daytime habit. I chased him all over the garden this morning to get this photo. There`s more info here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2003)

File.Name: 3811a_3810-093.jpg
Notes: Male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly visiting our garden. (Is that composite flower an Echinacea?) We also have a female fluttering around, and the two have been fluttering together. The males (but not the famales) have an amazing irridescent "dog face" pattern on their upper wings, but they hide it as soon as they land and fold their wings up. They are very strong fliers, but they are finally getting used to me following them. I have one fuzzy photo "in flight", but I`m waiting for something better to share. This is the most beautiful butterfly I know. It`s our California State Butterfly for good reason, but it looks like just another Sulphur unless you get the right view as it flutters by. I have a photo of the female here. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2003)

File.Name: 3811b_3810-084.jpg
Notes: Close shot of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly visiting our garden. (Is that composite flower an Echinacea?) He has an amazing irridescent "dog face" pattern hidden on his upper wings that I`m trying to photograph. I have a nice photo of the female here. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2003)

File.Name: 3811c_3731-135.jpg
Notes: Small butterfly in the Oregon woods working these wild (but weedy) Sweetpea (Lathyrus odoratus) flowers. I think it`s some kind of Hairstreak (Satyrium?), but my books aren`t very helpful about the underside of the wings. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2003)

File.Name: 3811d_3809-009.jpg
Notes: Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) in our garden feeding on Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii?). I`m shooting up into the sky, and I love what happened to the flower colors! I have another Monarch photo from the same morning here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2003)

File.Name: 3811e_3810-050.jpg
Notes: Is it a bumblebee? Is it a hummingbird? No! I`m pretty sure this is a Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis), also called a Bumblebee or Hummingbird Moth for good reasons. It`s a strange kind of Sphinx Moth (Sphingidae) that mimics a large bumblebee (about an inch long) and hovers like a hummingbird to suck nectar from flowers. This beauty breaks all the usual moth rules with clear wings and daytime habit. I chased him all over the garden to get this photo and this one in Bugs N` Critters. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2003)

File.Name: 3811f_3725-401.jpg
Notes: I think this is a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis trisris tristis) visiting a Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). It`s a lovely name for this dark brown (not black) skipper. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2003)

File.Name: 3811h_3804-358.jpg
Notes: Insect nymph. I think the plant is Pokeweed (Phytolacca americana), not common (yet) in California. (Chico, California, 4 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812a_3812-151.jpg
Notes: This is the back side of the Santa Barbara Courthouse where I have to (get to) report for jury duty for the next couple of weeks until school starts. Of course, that`s part of being a citizen. The rest of todays photos are from the courthouse garden or Alice Keck park a few blocks away. Expect more. (Santa Barbara, 12 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812b_3812-138.jpg
Notes: Look near the center to see the ant crawling in these flowers of some kind of horticultural Ginger (Zingiberaceae) at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. Think how big your garden is to such a small critter! (Santa Barbara, 12 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812c_3812-150.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Australian Bottlebrush (Callistemon citrinus?). (Santa Barbara, 12 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812d_3811-116.jpg
Notes: Distinctive column of joined stamens and anthers on a garden Hibiscus. (Santa Barbara, 11 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812e_3811-122.jpg
Notes: Secret places in a Water Lily flower. Reminds me of a Moebius comic. (Santa Barbara, 11 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812f_3812-135.jpg
Notes: The pretty red "petals" of Bougainvillea are really leaves. This shows the true flowers inside. I have another photo here. (Santa Barbara, 12 August 2003)

File.Name: 3812h_3811-117.jpg
Notes: Different worlds with Koi fish and water lilies at Alice Keck Park in Santa Barbara.
Locality: Different worlds with Koi fish and water lilies at Alice Keck Park in Santa Barbara.

File.Name: 3813a_3813-212.jpg
Notes: The clocktower and front entrance of the Santa Barbara Courthouse. I`m amazed by how much pink there is in the shadows. (Reflection from red tile roof?) I`m sitting on a jury for the next couple of weeks, but what a beautiful courthouse to sit in. The style is Spanish-Moorish-Hollywood-Revival (or something like that), built after the devastating 1925 earthquake leveled the old town. There`s some info here. Tourists and Brooks Institute of Photography students are always photographing the courthouse. I reckon I photograph these buildings just like I photograph my wildflowers, but I`d rather be in the woods! (Santa Barbara, 13 August 2003)

File.Name: 3813b_3813-221.jpg
Notes: Hallway at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. I was wedged against the wall for this handheld 1/10 second exposure. (Santa Barbara, 13 August 2003)

File.Name: 3813c_3813-227.jpg
Notes: Doorway at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. The door looks like dark chocolate! (Santa Barbara, 13 August 2003)

File.Name: 3813d_3813-235.jpg
Notes: Spiral staircase at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. (Santa Barbara, 13 August 2003)

File.Name: 3813e_3813-188.jpg
Notes: Looking up at the stonework on the front of the Santa Barbara Courthouse. (Santa Barbara, 13 August 2003)

File.Name: 3813f_3813-191.jpg
Notes: Looking up at a Bougainvillea vine climbing the front of the Santa Barbara Courthouse. (Santa Barbara, 13 August 2003)

File.Name: 3813h_3812-156.jpg
Notes: Funnel Web (Agelenidae?) spider web on our porch. This might be the entrance to hell for some unfortunate insects. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2003)

File.Name: 3814a_3814-417.jpg
Notes: New acorns on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia aka Q. dumosa). I`m still on jury duty, but I`m free until Monday, so I took a long walk. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3814b_3814-276.jpg
Notes: California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. latifolium aka Zauschneria californica). This is a beautiful late-flowering hummingbird plant that has just started flowering here on San Marcos Pass in our dry season. It`s interesting to compare it to the Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) shown here. The penstemon has the female stigma on top to rub on the hummer`s head, but the fuschia has it on the bottom to rub on the hummer`s chin! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; latifolium; Zauschneria californica; Keckiella cordifolia; stigma
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; latifolium; Zauschneria californica; Keckiella cordifolia; stigma

File.Name: 3814c_3814-385.jpg
Notes: Beautiful peeling bark of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). It feels so good to run your hand up the smooth branch and rub it all away! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3814d_3814-334.jpg
Notes: Elegant seed pods of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) opening to release the tiny seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3814e_3814-332.jpg
Notes: Very glandular flower heads of Tarweed (Madia elegans ssp. densifolia). The pretty composite flowers open at night and close at dawn, so I rarely see them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3814f_3814-375.jpg
Notes: A universe of lichens on a branch in the chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3814h_1506-001.jpg
Notes: Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the inside of the window screen in my study.
Locality: Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the inside of the window screen in my study.

File.Name: 3815a_3814-402.jpg
Notes: Pretty clown-colored detachable stem Gall on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia aka Q. dumosa). Plant galls are tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and other organisms which lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate. I think this one is caused by the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella). The larval wasps live inside until ready to breed. There are mysteries everywhere when you look close at nature! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)
CommonNames: Gall; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Q. dumosa; Cynipidae; Disholcaspis plumbella
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Q. dumosa; Cynipidae; Disholcaspis plumbella

File.Name: 3815b_3814-400.jpg
Notes:
Tiny detachable Saucer Gall on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia aka Q. dumosa). Plant galls are tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and other organisms which lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate. This particular gall is maybe the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andrecus gigas). The larval wasp lives in that circular bump in the center, which gives you an idea of the size of it. What a life -- like living in a giant burrito and only emerging to breed! ;=)

This is about as close as I can get with my Olympus. It`s a 500x500 crop from the original handheld "macro" photo on full zoom. The entire gall is about 1/8 inch across. The closer I look at nature, the more I see. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)
CommonNames: Saucer Gall; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps; Saucer Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Q. dumosa; Cynipidae; Andrecus gigas
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Q. dumosa; Cynipidae; Andrecus gigas

File.Name: 3815c_3814-316.jpg
Notes: Pretty lichens growing on a dead branch of Gray Pine (Pinus sabiniana). (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3815d_3814-297.jpg
Notes: Ripening seeds on Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare). It`s pretty and tasty, but it`s one of our most invasive weeds here in Santa Barbara. The plants (and all those seeds!) are following the road into the mountains. I have another photo of the flowers here. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3815e_3814-338.jpg
Notes: New growth on Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri). (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3815f_3814-250.jpg
Notes: Perfect Funnel Web (Agelenidae?) spider web decending into an old gopher hole. Look close for Dorothy and the Wicked Witch! I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3815g_3814-295.jpg
Notes: Bumblebee on Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare). (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3816a_3815-451.jpg
Notes: Orion in the morning sky and fall colors on Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). The season is turning. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2003)

File.Name: 3816b_3815-457.jpg
Notes: Most plants in the Rush family (Juncaceae) grow in wetlands, but this is a beautiful potrero of dry woodland with an understory of Wood Rush (Luzula comosa) blowing in the wind under the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2003)

File.Name: 3816c_3815-489.jpg
Notes: Developing seeds of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum). The fruit in the lower left is splitting into four separate seeds, like slicing an apple pie. That white fluffy stuff is a mass of woolly hairs, but it looks like foam or crystals. I have photos of the remarkable flowers here and here. It does ok in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2003)

File.Name: 3816d_3815-432.jpg
Notes: Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly resting on California Bedstraw (Gallium californicum ssp. flaccidum). The closed wings are almost perfect camouflage with the dead oak leaves on the ground. I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2003)

File.Name: 3816e_3816-007.jpg
Notes: Flight pattern of Golden Polistes (Polistes fuscates aurifer) paper wasps getting a drink at our little pond in the garden. They make the familiar umbrella-shaped paper-like nests that you find under eaves and porch rails. They look wicked with that narrow waist, but they never bother me. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3816f_3816-023.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly working Fuschia flowers in our garden. This butterfly knows the same trick as the Carpenter Bees. It enters into the calyx at the base of the flower to steal the nectar without pollinating the flower up front. I don`t notice many other insects on these flowers. Maybe they need that very long proboscis to get to the good stuff. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2003)
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus rutulus; Fuschia; very long proboscis
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus rutulus; Fuschia; very long proboscis

File.Name: 3816g_3815-420.jpg
Notes: Golden Polistes (Polistes fuscates aurifer) paper wasp getting a drink from a bowl in the garden. They make the familiar umbrella-shaped paper-like nests that you find under eaves and porch rails. They look wicked with that narrow waist, but they never bother me. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2003)

File.Name: 3817a_3808-448.jpg
Notes: My favorite tree in the perfect light. This is the view of a glowing Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree from my porch with the setting sun off to the left. I waited a week to see if the effect would get even better, but now it doesn`t happen at all. I searched my archives and found an almost identical shot from the same date a year ago, but now the foreground oaks have grown a bit more so it`s really different. I have a stumper about The Perfect Light. Think of all the perfect views that no one sees, like those falling trees that no one hears. It`s a lesson for all us photographers who try to capture these glances that every moment of every day is unique! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2003)
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; the same date; glances
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; the same date; glances

File.Name: 3817b_3816-076.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata). They don`t taste as good as they look! (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3817c_3817-090.jpg
Notes: Small cone of Smooth Scouring Rush (aka Horsetail) (Equisetum hyemale ssp. affine) in the little pond that catches the overflow from our hot/cold tub. I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2003)

File.Name: 3817d_3816-031.jpg
Notes: This large wasp is more than an inch long, probably a Thread-waisted Wasp (family Specinae, genus Sphex?). It`s visiting a milkweed in our garden. It appears to be chewing off the pollen sack from the flower, so it`s another robber. Philosopher Daniel Dennett introduced this wasp (or its kin) into recent debate about free will and determinism through its quality of "Sphexishness". Really, check here! (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3817e_3816-070.jpg
Notes: Beautiful leaves of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii). (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3817f_3816-066.jpg
Notes: Ripe seed heads of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) with the sun behind. You can shake the tiny seeds out and eat them as a kind of chia. Tasty, but they are NOT seeds if they move! (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3817g_3513-001.jpg
Notes: Fledgeling crow one of my students found on the ground last spring. She carried the bird to school everyday and cared for it like Mom, and one day the bird flew away for good. Really!
Locality: Fledgeling crow one of my students found on the ground last spring. She carried the bird to school everyday and cared for it like Mom, and one day the bird flew away for good. Really!

File.Name: 3818a_3817-108.jpg
Notes: The "golden rolling hills of California" - as Kate Wolf used to sing, bless her. Local author Stewart Edward White said it`s the gold of ripeness, not decay. It`s hard to fit a windy landscape into 500 pixels! (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2003)

File.Name: 3818b_3817-093.jpg
Notes: Western Ragweed (Ambrosia psilostachya) is a little-loved weedy native that has its charms in the right light. These flowers are not quite ripe. (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2003)

File.Name: 3818c_3817-095.jpg
Notes: Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) is a late blooming composite on the dry river bed that usually fuels a lot of insects. For some reason there was no insect activity yesterday evening. (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2003)

File.Name: 3818d_3816-047.jpg
Notes: A tattered Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly visiting California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasiculatum) flowers. As I get older and more tattered myself, I`ve come to appreciate these survivors! I have more Buckeye photos here and here. (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3818e_3814-292.jpg
Notes: Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) is pretty and tasty, but it`s one of our most invasive weeds here in Santa Barbara. The plants are following the roads into the mountains where I live. This is looking out over the city and ocean, with Santa Cruz Island beyond. It`s taking over there too. I have more photos here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2003)

File.Name: 3818f_3816-063.jpg
Notes: Hiking to a little-known swimming hole along a section of the local Santa Ynez River known as Paradise. Look closer to see all the plumes of invasive Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) taking over the river bed natives. Learn this weedy grass if you don`t know it. (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2003)

File.Name: 3818g_3810-074.jpg
Notes:
I`ve been chasing this male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly through our woods and garden for two weeks trying to get a shot with his wings open. He`s a strong flier and I`m convinced he`s teasing me.

This might be the best shot I`ll get, fuzzy as it is. It`s our California State Butterfly for good reason, with wonderful iridescence on the upper wings. I have more sharp photos (with wings folded) here, here, and here. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2003)

File.Name: 3819a_3819-227.jpg
Notes: Beautiful tower at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. I`m sitting on a jury, but what a beautiful courthouse to sit in. The style is Spanish-Moorish-Hollywood-Revival (or something like that), built after the devastating 1925 earthquake leveled the old town. I have more courthouse photos here (and previous). I reckon I photograph this building just like I photograph my wildflowers, but I`d rather be in the woods! (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3819b_3819-187.jpg
Notes: The Rose Window and reflections on tile wall at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3819c_3819-198.jpg
Notes: Looking up at a painted arch and ceiling at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. Natural light with my camera supported on a rail, F2.6 @ 1/40s. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3819d_3819-201.jpg
Notes: Ceiling of the amazing Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. No flash or tripod, but I rested my camera on a chair, F1.8 @ 1/5s. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)
CommonNames: Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room; Santa Barbara Courthouse

File.Name: 3819e_3819-210.jpg

CommonNames: Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room; Santa Barbara Courthouse

File.Name: 3819f_3819-194.jpg

File.Name: 3820a_3820-255.jpg
Notes: I`m still on jury duty, so I`m using my lunch breaks to photograph the beautiful Santa Barbara Courthouse. This is a detail shot of the painted ceiling of the amazing Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room. I have more courthouse photos if you click on the [previous] button. Natural light, but I brought my tripod today. (Santa Barbara, 20 August 2003)
CommonNames: Santa Barbara Courthouse; Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room

File.Name: 3820b_3820-253.jpg
Notes: Another corner of the amazing Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. The walls and ceiling are covered with murals and stencils. These murals show Spanish conquistadors and Father Junipero Serra who had their own idea of justice. Naural light, but I brought my tripod today. (Santa Barbara, 20 August 2003)
CommonNames: Old Board of Supervisors Assembly Room; Santa Barbara Courthouse

File.Name: 3820c_3819-237.jpg
Notes: Looking almost straight up at the ceiling of the front stairway at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. Natural light with my camera supported on a rail, F1.8 @ 1/13s. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3820d_3819-212.jpg
Notes: Painting on a concrete beam at the Santa Barbara Courthouse entrance. It`s an illusion! It looks like it comes down and out, but it really comes down and back under the beam. There`s another photo of the same beam here. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3820e_3819-158.jpg
Notes: The Rose Window and a lantern at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. Natural light with my camera supported on a bench, F6.3 @ 1/10s. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3820f_3819-247.jpg
Notes: Hallway of justice at the beautiful Santa Barbara Courthouse. The reflections look slippery to me! Natural light with my camera supported on a bench, F3.6 @ 1/6s. (Santa Barbara, 19 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822a_3821-288.jpg
Notes: Elegant geometry of an opening Dahlia, notice how the petals are folded. I`ve been sitting on jury duty all week in Santa Barbara, but I get to take long walks during my lunch breaks. Today`s photos are all garden flowers from the neighborhood. My duty is almost over... and school is about to begin. (Santa Barbara, 21 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822b_3821-291.jpg
Notes: Detail of Morning Glory (Ipomoea tricolor?) flower. The shadows in the center look like little dancing demons. (Santa Barbara, 21 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822c_3818-124.jpg
Notes: Unknown garden Poppy that looks like a yellow version of California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica), maybe a cultivar of E. caespitosa? (Santa Barbara, 18 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822d_3821-284.jpg
Notes: Real Scarlet Begonias (Begoniaceae). (Santa Barbara, 21 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822e_3818-126.jpg
Notes: The center of a Trumpet Vine flower (Bignoniaceae). (Santa Barbara, 18 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822f_3821-274.jpg
Notes: Unknown garden flower in shade, petals like wings. (Santa Barbara, 21 August 2003)

File.Name: 3822h_3821-305.jpg
Notes: Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus aurifer?) checking me out. (Santa Barbara, 21 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823a_3822-354.jpg
Notes: Rothrock`s Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) is a delicate plant I have to hunt for in shady canyons that hold some water at the end of our dry summer. The flowers are small but elegant. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823b_3822-430.jpg
Notes: Tiny flowers of Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii). The dried leaves make wonderful minty tea, and the plant gave the original name to San Francisco. It`s a low trailing perennial herb that is not very common here. I usually smell it before I see it. This specimen is in our native garden. It grows well from cuttings. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823c_3822-315.jpg
Notes: Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum) is a late-flowering composite with dandelion-like flowers that grows on shady banks and hangs into the light. It`s tough to photograph because the diffuse flowers appear on long wand-like stems that seem to move even when there is no wind. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823d_3822-363.jpg
Notes: Beautiful rose-like flower of Thornless Blackberry (Rubus ulmifolius inermis). This is a garden hybrid developed by Luther Burbank that is displacing the natives along San Jose Creek. I`ve never found any fruit. This is natural light from a sunbeam in just the right place. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823e_3822-419.jpg
Notes: I admit that Fountain Grass (Pennisetum setaceum) has beautiful feathery flower spikes that catch the sun just so. But it`s an invasive weed that is spreading along Highway 154 into our mountains. (Did CalTrans originally plant it??) So far, I only find it along the roads, but it`s become a major problem in Hawaii and other Pacific islands. It bugs me that it`s still being sold in local nurseries as an ornamental at the same time that big money is being spent to control it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823f_3822-431.jpg
Notes: Our wild Peppermint (Mentha xpiperita) is a non-native hybrid. I only find it occasionally here in the mountains. It has a short flower spike above leaves that attach to the stems with short stalks or petioles. It often droops like this when the sun is on it. Useful herb for tea. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3823h_3822-346.jpg
Notes: This little froglet of a Pacific Chorus Frog (Pseudacris regilla) aka Pacific Tree Frog (Hyla regilla) is less than a half-inch long. Now look at that tiny snail floating on the surface film next to it! (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)
CommonNames: Pacific Chorus Frog; Pacific Tree Frog

File.Name: 3824a_3823-210.jpg
Notes: Dodder (Cuscuta salina or C. californica?) is a strange native parasitic plant without roots or chlorophyll. It sucks the life from other plants. Here it is starting to take over Beachbur (Ambrosia chamissonis). It reminds me of the old Invasion of the Body Snatchers movie. I wouldn`t camp next to it! Beachbur has it`s own hazards. Don`t try to photograph it barefoot or you`ll learn how it got its name! This is a restored dune area near Haskell`s Beach. (Goleta, 23 August 2003)
ScientificName: Cuscuta salina; C. californica; Ambrosia chamissonis
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta salina; C. californica; Ambrosia chamissonis

File.Name: 3824b_3823-220.jpg
Notes: Dodder (Cuscuta salina or C. californica?) is a strange native parasitic plant without roots or chlorophyll. It sucks the life from other plants. Here it is taking over Beachbur (Ambrosia chamissonis). This is a restored dune area near Haskell`s Beach. (Goleta, 23 August 2003)
ScientificName: Cuscuta salina; C. californica; Ambrosia chamissonis
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta salina; C. californica; Ambrosia chamissonis

File.Name: 3824c_3823-131.jpg
Notes: Dodder (Cuscuta salina or C. californica?) is a strange parasitic plant without roots or chlorophyll. It sucks the life from other plants, in this case a barely-recognizable Beachbur (Ambrosia chamissonis). Dodder does have pretty little flowers related to morning-glories. This is on a restored dune area near Haskell`s Beach. (Goleta, 23 August 2003)
ScientificName: Cuscuta salina; C. californica; Ambrosia chamissonis; does
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta salina; C. californica; Ambrosia chamissonis; does

File.Name: 3824d_3823-222.jpg
Notes: Beach Evening Primrose (Camissonia cheiranthifolia ssp. suffruticosa) on a restored dune area near Haskell`s Beach. (Goleta, 23 August 2003)

File.Name: 3824e_3823-119.jpg
Notes: Saw-toothed Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa aka Haplopappus squarrosus) growing on a restored dune area near an estuary close to Haskell`s Beach. I wouldn`t be surprised if this shrub was on the bluffs, but it doesn`t seem right here on the sand just above the beach. I worry when "habitat restoration" goes beyond removing weeds to actually replanting natives. Lots of money is at stake in restoration efforts, and quick results are part of the contract. I`d rather let nature take her course. (Goleta, 23 August 2003)

File.Name: 3824f_3823-115.jpg
Notes: Beach Sand-Verbena (Abronia umbellata) on a restored dune area near Haskell`s Beach. (Goleta, 23 August 2003)

File.Name: 3824g_3823-017.jpg
Notes: Anise Swallowtail (Papilo zelicaon zelicaon) on a Milkweed in our garden. These butterflies have an interesting trick. They get to a flower and hover by only flapping their front wings. You can see what`s sharp and what`s blurred in the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2003)

File.Name: 3825a_3823-090.jpg
Notes: Anise Swallowtail (Papilo zelicaon) butterfly at the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in our garden. I have sharper photos, but I love those wings with the dreamy backlighting that look like stained glass. These swallowtails have a solid black area on their front wings where the Western Tiger Swallowtails (Papilio rutulus) have stripes. (See here to compare.) Despite the name, this beauty is native and lives on other native members of the Parsley family (Apiaceae) besides introduced Fennel. We have lots of butterflies in our garden right now. I reckon they`re "hilltopping", and I live on top of the hills! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2003)
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Butterfly Bush; Western Tiger Swallowtails
ScientificName: Papilo zelicaon; Buddleia davidii; Papilio rutulus; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilo zelicaon; Buddleia davidii; Papilio rutulus; Apiaceae

File.Name: 3825b_3823-063.jpg
Notes: Anise Swallowtail (Papilo zelicaon) butterfly hovering overhead at the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in our garden. These butterflies have an interesting trick. They get to a flower and hover by only flapping their front wings. You can see what`s sharp and blurred in the photo. They look fluttery when you see them in action, but it looks strange captured by the camera. I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2003)

File.Name: 3825c_3823-051.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly working the Fuschia in our garden. I have more photos here and in the archives. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2003)

File.Name: 3825d_3823-094.jpg
Notes: American Painted Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) working Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in our garden. You can tell this tattered butterfly has been around. The long two-toned proboscis working the flower is impressive, but notice the snout or horns on front (aka labial palps) that guide it! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2003)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis; Buddleia davidii; proboscis; labial palps
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis; Buddleia davidii; proboscis; labial palps

File.Name: 3825e_3823-085.jpg
Notes: There`s no mystery how Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) got it`s name. Here are American Painted Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) and Anise Swallowtail (Papilo zelicaon) butterflies sharing a flower cluster in our garden. These critters are delicate but feisty, and the peace didn`t last long! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2003)
CommonNames: Butterfly Bush; American Painted Lady; Anise Swallowtail
ScientificName: Buddleia davidii; Vanessa virginiensis; Papilo zelicaon
associatedTaxa: Buddleia davidii; Vanessa virginiensis; Papilo zelicaon

File.Name: 3825f_3822-395.jpg
Notes: A perfect Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly resting on dead oak leaves. He posed for me. Did he think he was hiding with his wings and "eyes" open like that? The green plant with four leaves in a whorl is California Bedstraw (Galium californicum ssp. flaccidum). These buckeyes are very common right now. I have another photo here and more in the archives. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3825g_3823-044.jpg
Notes: This is a two-group post. Here is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly working the Fuschia in our garden. There`s a close-up in the Texture group.
Locality: This is a two-group post. Here is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus rutulus) butterfly working the Fuschia in our garden. There`s a close-up in the Texture group.

File.Name: 3826a_3826-259.jpg
Notes: I think this is a Pacific Chorus Frog (Pseudacris regilla) aka Pacific Tree Frog (Hyla regilla). Our hot tub becomes our cold tub in summer, and we share it with the critters. I`ve come to recognize this particular treefrog because he has a yellow patch on his left side (your right). He`s always in about the same place in the tub, and we tolerate each other. Here he is hanging on the side of the redwood tub wishing I`d get it over with! I just posted another shot of the same treefrog taken over a month ago here. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2003)
CommonNames: Pacific Chorus Frog; Pacific Tree Frog

File.Name: 3826b_3719-063.jpg
Notes: I think this is a Pacific Chorus Frog (Pseudacris regilla) aka Pacific Tree Frog (Hyla regilla). Our hot tub becomes our cold tub in summer, and we share it with the critters. I`ve come to recognize this particular treefrog because he has that distinctive yellow patch on his left side. This is an old photo, but he`s still hanging on more than a month later, see here. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2003)
CommonNames: Pacific Chorus Frog; Pacific Tree Frog

File.Name: 3826c_3826-237.jpg
Notes: This tattered Behr`s Metalmark (Apodemia mormo virgulti) butterfly is only about the size of a honey bee. He`s resting on a native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden, but he was working the Oregano flowers nearby. The striped antennae (and the way he holds them together) are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2003)

File.Name: 3826d_3822-308.jpg
Notes: Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly resting on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana). These butterflies were introduced into California (along with weedy Wild Mustard (Brassica, etc.) maybe back in the Mission days. Now they are part of the wildlands. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2003)

File.Name: 3826e_3826-262.jpg
Notes: There are many kinds of moth-like Skipper (Megathymidae) butterflies, and I sure can`t tell them apart. This one is working the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2003)

File.Name: 3826f_3810-074.jpg
Notes:
I`ve been chasing this male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies through our woods and garden for weeks trying to get a shot with his wings open. He`s a strong flier and I`m convinced he`s teasing me.

I haven`t seen him for a while, so this might be the best shot I`ll get, fuzzy as it is. It`s our California State Butterfly for good reason, with wonderful iridescence on the upper wings. I have more sharp photos (with wings folded) here, here, and here. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2003)

File.Name: 3827a_3826-276.jpg
Notes: Not every acorn becomes a mighty oak, as shown by this Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia aka Q. dumosa) near home. One acorn became insect food, probably a weevil grub, you can see the exit hole. The others never developed. Interesting thought now that jury duty is done and school is just around the corner. I have another photo of fat happy acorns here! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2003)

File.Name: 3827b_3826-272.jpg
Notes: Elegant seed pods of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) still show the female stigma lobes at the tip. I have a photo of the flower with the start of a seed pod here. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2003)

File.Name: 3827c_3826-265.jpg
Notes: Harding Grass (Phalaris aquatica) is an introduced grass that is spreading along roads and into the woodland in our mountains. It`s invasive, but it sure is pretty with the sun behind it. There`s a rumor that it has mind-altering properties. Really, see here. I figure if enough people harvest it, maybe it will go away! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2003)

File.Name: 3827d_3827-304.jpg
Notes: This pretty little moth was trying to hide in a basket of tomatoes and chilies from the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2003)

File.Name: 3827e_3826-287.jpg
Notes:
The planet Mars at closest approach to Earth, 27 August 2003 at 1:30 AM PDT, full telephoto on a tripod, F2.6 @ 16 seconds. It`s still just a (over-exposed) red dot, so I enlarged the 30x30 pixel planet to 500x500 pixels. Kind of pretty. I also pasted the original size image in the upper right corner for comparison. I used a Black Frame Noise Reduction tool to despeckle the image. (See my previous image.)

I`m sure I can do better. Mars will be nearly as close for the next month at least, so there`s plenty of time to try again! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2003)

File.Name: 3827f_3826-291.jpg
Notes:
I did my duty as a science teacher last night and went out at 1:30 AM PDT to get a photo of Mars at closest approach. (See this NASA page.) My method was brute force: set my Olympus 2040Z digital camera up on a tripod at full telephoto and take the longest manual exposure possible, F2.6 @ 16 seconds.

The problem is that digital photo sensors fire off +/- random pixels over time. Astronomers use elaborate schemes to keep their cameras VERY COLD. Another way is to put your hand over the lens and take a "black frame" shot and then subtract it from your image using a program like the free MediaChance BlackFrame NR utility. This works because the hot pixels aren`t quite random, they will most likely be in the same place during a short period at the same temperature. (I have three hot pixels that show up in every photo.)

This is a 500x500 pixel section of my 16 second "black frame" exposure with the lens covered. Looks pretty busy for nothing! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2003)

File.Name: 3829a_3829-491.jpg
Notes: I had another encounter today with a Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis), also called a Bumblebee or Hummingbird Moth for good reasons. He`s hovering and sucking at the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in our garden. What control! I have more photos of these amazing creatures here, here, and here. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3829b_3829-486.jpg
Notes: American Painted Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) working the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in our garden. Notice how her proboscis is neatly coiled up between the horns or labial palps on her face. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2003)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis; Buddleia davidii; proboscis; labial palps
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis; Buddleia davidii; proboscis; labial palps

File.Name: 3829c_3829-479.jpg
Notes: American Painted Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) and Painted Gentleman butterflies! They are working the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in our garden. Note the red and orange color difference. They were getting frisky together, so I figure they are related. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2003)
CommonNames: American Painted Lady; Painted Gentleman; Butterfly Bush

File.Name: 3829d_3829-443.jpg
Notes: Pretty little butterfly, maybe a Common Hairstreak (Strymon melinus puducus), feeding on Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). Follow that wandering tongue crossing its front leg! The late-flowering composite shrubs on the dry river wash are full of insects right now. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)
ScientificName: Strymon melinus puducus; Lepidospartum squamatum; full
associatedTaxa: Strymon melinus puducus; Lepidospartum squamatum; full

File.Name: 3829e_3829-368.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Pond Damselfly (Coenagrionidae) resting on a flower of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3829f_3829-331.jpg
Notes: Tiny wasp on a flower of Hairy Goldenaster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echioides aka Chrysopsis villosa) growing on the dry river bed of the Santa Ynez River. The river is just a trickle now with a few great spring-fed swimming holes. It`s like a desert on the flood plain away from the pools, but these late-summer composites keep the insects in nectar. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3829g_3829-489.jpg
Notes: Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) hovering and sucking at the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in our garden. This photo is looking through its fast-beating clear wings! It`s a strange kind of Sphinx Moth (Sphingidae) that mimics a bumblebee and hovers like a hummingbird to suck nectar from flowers. This beauty breaks all the usual moth rules with clear wings and daytime habit. I have another new photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2003)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Butterfly Bush; Sphinx Moth

File.Name: 3830a_3829-398.jpg
Notes: Twisted flower bud of Toloache (Datura wrightii). I`m surprised how yellow this is. The large white flowers open at night. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3830b_3829-387.jpg
Notes: California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) with just a bit of extra sunlight coming into the shade. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3830c_3829-409.jpg
Notes: I found this Croton (Croton californicus) in the dry woodland near White Rock in the upper Santa Ynez River valley. Usually it grows on stabilized dunes near the ocean to the north, so this was a surprise. This species is dioecious with separate male and female plants. This one is male with stamens. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3830d_3829-434.jpg
Notes: Not a remarkable photo, but it is remarkable that this Great Basin Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata ssp. tridentata) is growing in the woodland on a dry river flat near Santa Barbara instead of the interior desert. It`s not common here. The few plants I`ve found look very different from their desert kin, softer and whiter, with no trunk. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3830e_3829-357.jpg
Notes: Seed head of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum). I have a photo of the flower here. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3830f_3829-346.jpg
Notes: You have to know California sages to appreciate this photo. This is a natural hybrid between White Sage (Salvia apiana) and Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla). It has the usual leaves of the former and seed heads in balls like the latter. Both parents were growing nearby. There`s a stumper here: if the plants can cross, then why do the separate species still exist? (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3831a_3829-497.jpg
Notes: The first seeds escaping from the pod on the Milkweed in our garden. It`s a good image to begin a new school year! Meetings tomorrow (laboring on Labor Day), first day of school on Tuesday, a school camping trip to Big Sur on Wednesday. Not bad! I`ll get back here next weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2003)

File.Name: 3831b_3829-320.jpg
Notes: Pretty Willow roots (maybe Salix Lasiolepis?) growing into a pool at the river. These roots can grow into your spring box and pipes and stop your water supply! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3831c_3829-373.jpg
Notes: Cocklebur (Xanthium strumarium) is a "native weed" with typical composite flowers (above) and seed pods with remarkable hooked spines (below). It`s said to be the original inspiration for velcro. Sometimes it`s easier to throw away socks than to untangle them. Dogs are a bigger problem! This is near the river, but it also grows along roads up in the mountains. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3831d_3829-448.jpg
Notes: The old rule is that Sedges (maybe Cyperus eragrostis?) have edges on leaves and stems. You can see them in this photo. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3831e_3829-339.jpg
Notes: We have native Tarragon (Artemisia dracunculus) growing in California, but it doesn`t have much taste, even when dried. This is a foliose insect gall on a flowering plant. It`s not in my gall book, but it might be from a Gall Midge (Cecidomiidae). Insect galls are fascinating and not well-known. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)
ScientificName: Artemisia dracunculus; foliose insect gall; Cecidomiidae
associatedTaxa: Artemisia dracunculus; foliose insect gall; Cecidomiidae

File.Name: 3831f_3829-459.jpg
Notes: New gourds on Calabazilla (Cucurbita foetidissima). The species name tells how it smells and tastes. The orange-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, tools, and rattles. It`s not common. This plant/colony near Paradise Camp is huge, covering 1/2 acre or more. I wonder if it goes back to the old days. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2003)

File.Name: 3901a_3901-024.jpg
Notes: Kissing? Naw, that`s the wrong end. This pair of Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillars were stripping the same leaf of Milkweed in our garden early this morning - at the bottom of the leaf. It`s interesting how the hind colors look opposite. I was up early for a day of school planning and cleanup. The first real day of school is Tuesday, and we have a three-day camping trip to Big Sur on Wednesday. Not bad! I`ll get back here next weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2003)

File.Name: 3901b_3901-029.jpg
Notes: Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillar stripping the Milkweed in our garden. That`s the head end on the right with the longer horns and short feet. No problem. Gardens are for butterflies too, and these milkweeds are already making seeds! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2003)

File.Name: 3901c_3901-026.jpg
Notes: We have babies! This Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillar left a "package" on a leaf of the Milkweed in our garden that he`s stripping. Scat, frass, or (*your_own_name*) it`s no problem. Gardens are for butterflies too! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2003)

File.Name: 3901d_3901-032.jpg
Notes: The back side of this Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on a Black-Eyed Susan (Rudbeckia hirta?) flower in our garden has a gnarly face to scare away preditors. Pretty cool adaptation! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2003)

File.Name: 3901e_3901-037.jpg
Notes: The business side of a perfectly camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on a Black-Eyed Susan (Rudbeckia hirta?) flower in our garden. I waited a long time for some action, but no luck for either of us. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906a_3904-346.jpg
Notes: Seaside Daisy (Erigeron glaucus) on the bluff at Sand Dollar Beach. We had our first week of Dunn Middle School at this perfect beach and the Plaskett Creek Campground just across the road. I`m starting a new DMS fotolog. I was pretty busy taking pictures as a teacher, but I did find some time to be a naturalist as well. The species are similar to home, but the mix is very different! (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906b_3905-459.jpg
Notes: Leaves of Red Flowering Currant (Ribes sanguineum var. glutinosum) starting to show fall colors. (Big Sur, 5 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906c_3904-357.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries of Red Flowering Currant (Ribes sanguineum var. glutinosum). They don`t taste very good. (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906d_3904-361.jpg
Notes: New foliage of a young Redwood (Sequoia sempervirens) tree near our campsite at Plaskett Creek. (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906e_3905-423.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Common Aster (Aster chilensis) still wet with morning dew. (Big Sur, 5 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906f_3904-261.jpg
Notes: Green cone on Monterey Pine (Pinus radiata). The brown scars have fantastic shapes like rabbits or flying dragons. (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3906h_3904-292.jpg
Notes: We found this Garter Snake (Thamnophis sirtalis?) on the sand at Sand Dollar Beach near a wet seep on the bluff. I was careful because of his triangular head and aggressive behavior as he hissed and tried to strike. (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3907a_3904-377.jpg
Notes: Watching the Sun set into the ocean framed by Monterey Pine (Pinus radiata) trees. This is a closed-cone pine. The cones (in principle) stay closed on the tree until toasted by fire to open and release their seeds. Look close on the branch to the right of the sun to see a line of old cones. I have a closer photo of a pine cone here. (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3907b_3903-232.jpg
Notes: Huge feathery plumes of Pampas Grass (Cortaderia jubata or a close relative). This seems to be a terrible invasive weed on the Big Sur coast, especially on disturbed slide areas along the highway. There`s more info here and here. Despite all the problems, this non-native invader is still sold in local nurseries. That`s not right. (Big Sur, 3 September 2003)

File.Name: 3907c_3904-296.jpg
Notes: Common Monkey Flower (Mimulus guttatus) growing on a wet seep on the bluff at Sand Dollar Beach. The ruffled calyces look like little cookie cutters! (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3907d_3905-429.jpg
Notes: Coastal Sagebrush (Artemisia californica) is another one of those composites with flowers that don`t look like flowers at first. They have small heads of disk flowers without the usual petal-like ray flowers. These aromatic shrubs have herbal uses. (Big Sur, 5 September 2003)
ScientificName: Artemisia californica; composites; disk flowers; ray flowers
