associatedTaxa: Artemisia californica; composites; disk flowers; ray flowers

File.Name: 3907e_3905-432.jpg
Notes: Flowers and twisted buds of Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia?). These flowers have much stronger color than our Santa Barbara plants. (Big Sur, 5 September 2003)

File.Name: 3907f_3904-354.jpg
Notes: Small wasp on Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertiflorum). (Big Sur, 4 September 2003)

File.Name: 3908a_3907-028.jpg
Notes: Beautiful old leaf on a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree at the end of the season. These evergreen trees develop their new leaves at the same time as they drop the old ones. New flowers are scarce in our summer-dry woods now, waiting for rain, but the old tattered leaves are so rich with character and beauty. Do you know the remarkable portraits in Mashuga`s Fotolog? (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3908b_3907-051.jpg
Notes: Old leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) Starting to show color at the end of the season. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3908c_3907-021.jpg
Notes: Pretty leaves of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) at the end of the season. I reckon every leaf tells a story, but this one has already lost a full chapter of its trilogy. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3908d_3907-016.jpg
Notes: Leaves of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) turning a deep color at the end of the season. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3908e_3907-050.jpg
Notes: Leaf of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) at the end of the season, but still looking good. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3908f_3907-012.jpg
Notes: Leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) at the end of the season. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3909a_3907-046.jpg
Notes: Nearly ripe seed pod of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) with the style still attached. I have more photos of the same plant here and here, and more if your search the archive. I`m glad there are new seeds developing. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3909b_3907-047.jpg
Notes: Nearly ripe seed pod of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in our canyon. I have more photos of the same plant here and here, and more if your search the archive. I`m glad there are new seeds developing. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3909c_3907-013.jpg
Notes: Ripe fruit of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans). They taste bitter. I have more photos of the flowers in the archives. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3909d_3907-011.jpg
Notes: Foliage of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) aka Oregon Myrtle and California Laural. It`s still our dry time here in central California, but these trees are getting buds ready to flower when the rains come. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3909e_3907-023.jpg
Notes: New leaves on a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree. They are getting new leaves at the same time as they drop the old ones. Compare this photo of the same tree. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3909f_3907-043.jpg
Notes: Soft leaves of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) with nice backlighting. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2003)

File.Name: 3912a_3909-057.jpg
Notes: Looking into an egg. You can see shell and membrane(s), white and yolk. The whitish spot on top of the yolk is the germinal disk where an embryo might develop. The fuzzy patch on the right is one of the chalazae that support the yolk like springs on each end. (It must have slipped to the side when the egg was broached on top.) We`re starting the school year studying eggs in my Dunn Middle School science classes. (Los Olivos, 9 September 2003)

File.Name: 3912b_3909-087.jpg
Notes: Separate egg white into a cup and blow bubbles with a skinny straw. It`s a coarse meringue with beautiful geometry. We`re starting the school year studying eggs in my Dunn Middle School science classes. (Los Olivos, 9 September 2003)

File.Name: 3912c_3912-301.jpg
Notes: A close shot of the pretty yellow aphids grazing on the Milkweed in our garden. They do some damage, and so do the Monarch caterpillars. We tolerate them both. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2003)

File.Name: 3912d_3912-305.jpg
Notes: Pretty yellow aphids on the Milkweed in our garden. They do some damage, and so do the Monarch caterpillars. We tolerate them both. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2003)

File.Name: 3912e_3912-315.jpg
Notes: Ripening seeds of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in a beam of late afternoon sunlight. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2003)

File.Name: 3912f_3910-131.jpg
Notes: Almost full Moon and the bright planet Mars rising above the trees at my home. They were closer together the two nights before, but lost in the fog. Full telephoto on a tripod, F2 @ 1 second exposure. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913g_3913-142.jpg
Notes: Lichens and an oak apple gall on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia aka Q. dumosa). This is the peak of our dry season here in central California. I'm ready for rain and mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913a_3913-128.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in afternoon backlight. This is a late-flowering hummingbird flower that is at its peak now. There's nothing like school to appreciate a weekend! I took a long walk starting at home today and took many photos of these flowers and others. I'll post a clearer shot tomorrow, but this photo (with no Photoshop help) stood out. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913b_3913-062.jpg
Notes: Insect galls on leaves of Skunkbrush aka Basket Bush or Squaw Bush (Rhus trilobata). It's a poison oak relative, but the middle leaf does not have a separate stalk and it has a distinctive smell. It was one of the most useful plants for local native Americans. These galls may be caused by leaf gall psyllid insects. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913c_3913-068.jpg
Notes: Tiny wasp on flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913d_3913-087.jpg
Notes: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) is a local endemic that is more interesting than beautiful. The flowers open in early morning and are a bit wilted by the time I got to them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913e_3913-174.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Cudweed-Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia, aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia which feels so good to say). It's another of our late-summer-flowering composites. There are many forms of this plant that grow from the beach to the mountain tops. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3913f_3913-180.jpg
Notes: Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa ssp. grindelioides?) growing on sandstone outcrop. This plant is growing from a crack in the rock. It's remarkable that it waits until the end of our long summer-dry season to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3914a_3913-133.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium). I posted another photo yesterday. The flowers have sticky white pollen, and you can see that some grains have found the long female stigmas. I discovered a new stumper. The flowers look red to my eye, but the backlit petals always look yellow or orange in my digital camera. Why? (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3914b_3913-029.jpg
Notes: Horizontal spikes of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). It's another late-summer composite that is just starting to flower at the end of our season. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3914c_3913-042.jpg
Notes: FLowers of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana). "Wort" is the old English name for a plant, so this is the mug plant. It was used for brewing beer before the use of hops and is still(?)used for strong beers like porter. There's some info here. The tea is bitter and astringent. Many locals swear by it as a cure for poison oak. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3914d_3913-222.jpg
Notes: Plummer's Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) is a southern California endemic. It's named for Ms. Sarah A. Plummer who lived in Goleta and has nothing to do with plumbing. This species is dioecious with separate male and female plants. This one is male with stamens. I've learned that "rare" in botany usually means rarely common. It's quite common here. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3914e_3913-164.jpg
Notes: Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) is an odd native plant related to Indian Paint Brush (Castilleja). The small white flowers are hidden in the leafy bracts and are probably done by now. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3914f_3913-059.jpg
Notes: Flowering plume of invasive Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) along our road. It took me a while to appreciate this introduced grass because it has a native "look" to it, but check this photo to see what it's doing on the local river bed. Learn this weedy grass if you don`t know it, and kill it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3915a_3915-389.jpg
Notes: Long-Horned Grasshopper (Tettigoniidae) on the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in our garden. This hopper is related to Katydids. The long antennae are very different from the short antennae of true grasshoppers like this one. I love those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2003)

File.Name: 3915b_3914-330.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a Dahlia flower in our garden. I think I know where the hive is along our creek, but it's too high to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2003)

File.Name: 3915c_3914-326.jpg
Notes: This grasshopper (Acrididae) is an unexpected pollinator on the Dahlia in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2003)

File.Name: 3915d_3915-359.jpg
Notes: California Sister (Limenitis bredowii) butterfly resting on the ground in our garden. These butterflies rarely visit our garden. I usually see them fluttering high in the oaks. Do they feed at all? (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2003)

File.Name: 3915e_3915-375.jpg
Notes: It's remarkable how flat a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) can become with wings folded. We have a pair of them hanging out on the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii)in our garden, and we have caterpillars on the Milkweed nearby. A garden is a wonderful thing! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2003)

File.Name: 3915f_3915-401.jpg
Notes: This Corn Earworm (Helicoverpa zea) on fresh corn from the garden was an uninvited guest to dinner tonight. He didn't stay. These become brown moths when they get a chance. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2003)

File.Name: 3922a_3920-013.jpg
Notes: One Side makes you Bigger, the other makes you small... We have many new Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillars stripping the Milkweed in our garden. I've been busy with school and sick with an unseasonable cold that won't go away. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2003)
ScientificName: One Side makes you Bigger, the other makes you small...; Danaus plexippus
associatedTaxa: One Side makes you Bigger, the other makes you small...; Danaus plexippus

File.Name: 3922b_3920-006.jpg
Notes: A new generation of Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillars stripping the Milkweed in our garden. I have another photo here. Milkweeds are remarkable for the unique insects they support that live no where else. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2003)

File.Name: 3922c_3920-034.jpg
Notes: The pretty yellow aphids grazing the Milkweed in our garden are starting to change into winged forms. These critters have not spread to any other garden plants. I have another photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2003)

File.Name: 3922d_3921-069.jpg
Notes: Yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) on a yellow Milkweed in our garden. These camouflaged preditors hide in the flowers to catch the unwary, but sometimes the hunter becomes the prey! See the previous two photos. (Spider-Wasp sequence, 1 of 3) (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2003)

File.Name: 3922e_3920-025.jpg
Notes: This Sphecidae Mud Dauber wasp (maybe Sceliphron caementarium?) is about to deliver the coup de grace to a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?). The hunter became the prey. (Spider-Wasp sequence, 2 of 3) (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2003)
ScientificName: Sphecidae; Sceliphron caementarium; coup de grace; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Sphecidae; Sceliphron caementarium; coup de grace; Thomisidae

File.Name: 3922f_3920-026.jpg
Notes: This flew into a photo I was about to take, but it took me a while to figure out what I had. This is a Sphecidae Mud Dauber wasp (maybe Sceliphron caementarium?) holding a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) in a fatal embrace. Follow body parts and count legs to understand. The hunter became the prey. (Spider-Wasp sequence, 3 of 3) (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2003)

File.Name: 3928a_3927-041.jpg
Notes: This pretty insect gall on the underside of a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) leaf reminds me of a woolly bear caterpillar. I believe these are caused by the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus). This furry mass is actually a colony of separate galls. Like many gall insects, these tiny wasps have a complicated life cycle alternating between a unisexual generation like this and a sexual generation with solitary leaf galls. Today's photos are all insect galls. These are tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and a few other organisms that lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate. Flowers are getting scarce in our woods, but these galls are fascinating and sometimes beautiful. The only book I know of is Ronald A. Russo, "Plant Galls of the California Region" (1979), unfortunately out of print. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Crystalline Gall Wasp; Gall Wasps

File.Name: 3928b_3927-021.jpg
Notes: Oaks have acorns instead of juicy fruit. This "oak apple" type insect gall on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) is something different. I believe these are caused by the California Gall Wasp (Andricus californicus), a species of 1/8 inch wasps known only from parthenogenic females. You can cut a gall open and find the hard capsules of several wasp larvae inside. These succulent galls become hard and dry. (See previous photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3928c_3927-052.jpg
Notes: An "oak apple" type insect gall on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). I believe these are caused by the California Gall Wasp (Andricus californicus). This gall is hard and dry unlike the younger galls that are succulent and juicy. There are no exit holes yet. (See next photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3928d_3927-016.jpg
Notes: Pretty detachable stem gall on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). I think this one is caused by the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella). The solitary wasp larva lives inside until ready to breed. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp

File.Name: 3928e_3927-048.jpg
Notes: Stem gall on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). I believe these group galls are caused by unisexual generations of the Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola). They also have alternating sexual generations with completely different solitary galls on leaf buds. Most oak galls are detachable but these large woody galls are part of the stem. Sometimes I find old galls with dozens of exit holes. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3928f_3927-106.jpg
Notes: Leaf galls on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis). The abnormal growth is caused by a colony of tiny aphids (Tamalia coweni?) that live on secretions within the folded leaf. If you unfold a leaf, you can see the aphids within. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3929a_3927-009.jpg
Notes: Banded Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (Argiope trifasciata) on it's web. These are beautiful large spiders an inch or more across that pose like perfect models if you keep your distance. I'm just starting to notice them in the woods now. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3929b_3925-233.jpg
Notes: Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly. They look a lot like our California Sister (Limenitis bredowii) (see here), but the orange patch extends to the edge of the forewing. They act differently and hang out near the river willows instead of the mountain oaks. The geometry of the white patches is beautiful. (Santa Ynez River, 25 September 2003)

File.Name: 3929c_3922-105.jpg
Notes: Leafy green Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on my lampshade at night. They are attracted to light. It's fun to watch them crawl across the TV screen like 1950s movie monster insects! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3929d_3922-100.jpg
Notes: Small Preying Mantis, maybe the Minor Ground Mantis (Litaneitria minor?), on our window screen at night, shot with flash. I'm sure he was looking at me with those big purple eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2003)

File.Name: 3929e_3923-124.jpg
Notes: A clutch of tiny insect eggs and tiny flowers of Willow Herb (Epilobium ciliatum). (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 September 2003)

File.Name: 3929f_3925-221.jpg
Notes: Frog meets human and vice versa. This little frog at our swimming hole had bright yellow legs underneath so I first thought it might be an endangered Foothill Yellow-legged Frog (Rana boylii), but I think the toe pads and dark eye stripe peg it as a Pacific Treefrog (Hyla regilla. There were lots of them despite the low water! (Santa Ynez River, 25 September 2003)
ScientificName: Rana boylii), but I think the toe pads and dark eye stripe peg it as a Pacific Treefrog (Hyla regilla; lots
associatedTaxa: Rana boylii), but I think the toe pads and dark eye stripe peg it as a Pacific Treefrog (Hyla regilla; lots

File.Name: 3a13a_3927-110.jpg
Notes: Strange three-petal flowers of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum). This plant is growing as a parasite on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). These interesting plants are dioecious with separate male and female plants, but I didn't check and I can't tell. Ants like the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003) I've been busy with school, family, and stumper stuff. (See links) It's good to get back here!

File.Name: 3a13b_3927-095.jpg
Notes: Western Ragweed (Ambrosia psilostachya) is a composite related to sunflower, though you have to study the flowers to see the relationship. It's a weedy native that I fight in our garden, but it is beautiful in the right light. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a13c_3927-049.jpg
Notes: A single Rose hip on California Wild Rose (Rosa californica). It's been a dry summer in central California, and most natives are producing few seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a13d_3927-033.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis). They are tasty if you just suck on them and spit out the seeds and skin. Look close and you'll see that other critters like them too. I'm starting to find animal scat made up entirely of these berries. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a13e_3927-022.jpg
Notes: Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) is sometimes called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves. These fully formed buds will wait until spring to open. I've noticed that many of our native shrubs and trees have buds ready just waiting for winter rain. Me too! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a13f_3927-012.jpg
Notes: Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) showing that fall is here. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a16a_3a14-073.jpg
Notes: Large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) clutching her egg sack. She sat motionless on this Zinnia in our school garden for days, but she finally disappeared with her treasure before they hatched. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a16b_3a14-081.jpg
Notes: Large Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) resting on her web. These impressive spiders are about the size of a quarter, and they spin strong webs that can stretch across trails and dirt roads. It's creepy to run into a web at night and wonder where the spider is! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a16c_3a14-076.jpg
Notes: Beetles on flowers of Golden-Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides), one of our many fall-flowering composites. So many beetles, so little time! The short wing covers and protruding abdomen suggest a Blister Beetle (Meloidae). (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a16d_3a07-087.jpg
Notes: Newly emerged chick and several more on the way in our incubator at school today! We've been studying eggs in my science classes. I pose the question: what is the PROBLEM that the egg is the SOLUTION to? Part of the design is to be strong enough for a hen to sit on and weak emough for a tiny chick to peck out of. But it's not easy! (DMS, 15 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a16e_3a13-035.jpg
Notes: Newly hatched chick at school today with refletions from sandman44 poster about day-by-day egg development. (DMS, 15 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a16f_3a03-340.jpg
Notes: We have blessed events happening in my classroom at school! Tiny chicks are hatching in our incubator. The poster that shows day-by-day egg development was a gift from "Uncle Sandman44", thanks again! (DMS, 15 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a22a_3a22-054.jpg
Notes: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. Most mushroom books say it's edible and "foolproof", but don't trust it in California! I've gotten sick from it and so have several friends. It's one of our first mushrooms here in central California, despite our recent 95+ degree hot and dry weather. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 3a22b_3a22-058.jpg
Notes: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom exuding drops of fluid. This beautiful fungus causes a heart rot that hollows out its host Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It's one of our first mushrooms despite the recent hot weather. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Laetiporus sulphureus; heart rot; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Laetiporus sulphureus; heart rot; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 3a22c_3a22-072.jpg
Notes: Close shot of a Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the early morning light. It's one of our first mushrooms despite the recent hot weather. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 3a22d_3a22-099.jpg
Notes: It's been 95+ degrees this week, so I was suprised to find these pretty Bolbitius vitellinus mushrooms on the lawn at school. (DMS, 22 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a22e_3a20-015.jpg
Notes: I finally found the Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) and her clutch of babies hiding in the Zinnias in our school garden. She's a tough model and a smart Mom because she's usually in the shade and by herself on the other side of the plant. I'll keep trying. (DMS, 22 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a22f_3a22-100.jpg
Notes: The chicks we hatched at school are doing fine and getting lots of attention. There are more photos at http://www.fotolog.net/dms. (DMS, 22 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25a_3a23-268.jpg
Notes: Old and new Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). You can see the heart-rot this fungus causes in oak trees. It looks like someone trimmed last year's fruiting body. Most mushroom books say it`s edible and "foolproof", but don`t trust it here in California! I`ve gotten sick from it and so have several friends. It`s the first real mushroom I've seen here in central California, despite this week's 95+ degree hot and dry weather. I have more photos in my archive (dated 10/23/03). (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 3a25b_3a23-263.jpg
Notes: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Look close and you can see it exuding a few drops of fluid. Usually this mushshroom grows in neat yellow shelves, but this one looks more like a brain. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 3a25c_3923-138.jpg
Notes: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) exuding drops of fluid despite the recent hot weather. I think the pretty reflection is the grain of the cut wood it's growing from. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 3a25d_3a22-179.jpg
Notes: It's been 95+ degrees this week, so I was suprised to find these pretty Bolbitius vitellinus mushrooms on the lawn at school. They didn't last long. (DMS, 22 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25e_3a22-176.jpg
Notes: Starfish and two kinds of sea anemone at a secret beach between Gaviota and Refugio. (Gaviota Coast, 23 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25f_3923-119.jpg
Notes: A secret beach between Gaviota and Refugio. The rock is layered Monterey Shale which is uplifted to nearly vertical. You can see the layers on the cliffs. The layers make interesting long tide pools when worn down by waves. (Gaviota Coast, 23 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25g_3a22-075.jpg
Notes: Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) is a weedy native that grows in bone-dry waste areas, but the small flowers are remarkable up close. You can find this plant with your nose because the vinegar smell is so strong! The flower is engineered like a hinge. A heavy bumblebee lands on the inviting flat petal in front, which brings down the stamens and pistil to its back. It's a cousin of our beautiful Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) native shrub. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25h_3a22-077.jpg
Notes: Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) going to seed. The dandelion-like seeds just starting to pop are about as close to snow as we had this week, with a record-setting 95+ degree heat wave and growing wildfire danger. The humble Coyote Bush is often dismissed as a weed, but I think it's one of our most important native plants for plant succession because it provides a sheltered seed bed for oak and other seedlings. When ranchers and cattle clear it out, the oak seedlings don't have a chance. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25i_3a22-056.jpg
Notes: White Flowers of a female Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea). This native composite shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants. It grows in waste places and is often dismissed as a weed, but the flowers keep late season insects alive and it provides important shelter for birds and oak tree seedlings. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a25j_3a22-099.jpg
Notes: Flowers of a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) have a distinct yellow color. This native composite shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants. It grows in waste places and is often dismissed as a weed, but the flowers keep late season insects alive and it provides important shelter for birds and oak tree seedlings. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a26a_3a25-104.jpg
Notes: Seed capsules of Weed's Mariposa (Calochortus weedii var. vestus). I have photos of the remarkable flowers in the archives dated 7/19/03. By the shadows, I can tell there are still some seeds left in the pods. May they live long and prosper! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a26b_3a25-014.jpg
Notes: Oak Apple insect galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Remember that oaks produce acorns! These "fruit" are caused by tiny Cynipidae gall wasps, maybe the Live Oak Gallfly (Callirhytis pomiformis), that insert eggs and a dose of RNA to re-program the tree to produce this special gall that nourishes the larvae within. You can see by the exit holes that this strategy works! Or are all those holes made by other predators going in? (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Gallfly

File.Name: 3a26c_3a25-036.jpg
Notes: Dandelion-like seed heads of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora). This is a weedy native that is common along roads here in the Santa Barbara mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a26d_3a25-067.jpg
Notes: Resin dripping from cone of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge above my home. These massive cones can be a foot long and weigh several pounds. Don't camp under these trees! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a26e_3a25-045.jpg
Notes: Some natives like this Chaparral Nightshade )Solanum xanti) still have a few late flowers despite the hot dry weather. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a26f_3a25-095.jpg
Notes: Waxy buds of Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) are fully formed now, though they won't open until early spring. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a27a_3a25-023.jpg
Notes: Large quarter-size Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) working a late summer flower of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. latifolium aka Zauschneria californica). It sure looks like he's pollinating, but he's actually cheating the flower by taking the nectar from the base and bypassing the fertile stamens and stigma up front. Hummingbirds do it right. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Epilobium canum; latifolium; Zauschneria californica
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Epilobium canum; latifolium; Zauschneria californica

File.Name: 3a27b_3a25-032.jpg
Notes: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) is a local endemic that is making seeds now, but it still has a few flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a27c_3a25-048.jpg
Notes: These flower buds on Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) will expand into beautiful catkins as soon as we get some rain instead of this &!$#@ dangerous fire weather. Many local shrubs have buds now despite months of summer drought in our California Mediterranean climate. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a28a_3a28-154.jpg
Notes: Fire weather! This was the sunrise as I left for school this morning. We've been spared this time, so far. The smoke is from the huge wildfires raging south of us. Our local fires were controlled because the Santa Ana winds didn't happen here in Santa Barbara County. I'm hoping for rain. It's time. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October, 2003)

File.Name: 3a29a_3a25-076.jpg
Notes: Colorful pattern of ripe and the last new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum ssp. foliolosum). (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a29b_3a25-123.jpg
Notes: These pretty autumn flowers of the composite Cudweed-Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia have caught the thistle-down seeds from some other composite. I love these late-season natives that keep our dry woods in flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a29c_3913-157.jpg
Notes: Acrobatic seed pods of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) after they have opened. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a31a_3a25-003.jpg
Notes: Rain drops on the pine needles at school. What a sudden change in the weather from 95+ degree fire weather two days ago to almost an inch of cold rain today. Bad timing for Halloween, but the first rain after summer is always a blessing. (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a31b_3a25-012.jpg
Notes: This ripe Habanero Chile in our garden is hotter than it looks. We had a small harvest this year, but good heat! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a31c_3a25-083.jpg
Notes: A single acorn on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This summer was dry, and last year was very dry. Acorns are scarce. It will be a hard time for the wildlife in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a31d_3921-079.jpg
Notes: Snowball or Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) has odd breast-like stipules at the base of the leaves. This also has new flower buds getting ready for spring. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2003)

File.Name: 3a31e_3913-197.jpg
Notes: The beautiful tri-colored berries of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) are almost all gone now. Birds and animals love them, though they are a powerful natural laxative. Yes, I do find scat containing almost nothing else. That's adaptation! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2003)

File.Name: 3a31f_3a31-448.jpg
Notes: Large nuts of Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus). This is a very beautiful deciduous native with an odd scattered distribution here in the Santa Barbara Mountains and around the world. This photo is from a month ago. The nuts are just about gone now. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2003)

File.Name: 3b01a_3b01-134.jpg
Notes: Looking north at the ruins of Knapp's Castle and the Santa Barbara backcountry beyond. "The castle" is the ruin of a mansion that was once owned by opera singer Lotte Lehman, but was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. Only a few stone walls and chimneys remain. Great views, especially after the air was scrubbed of smoke by the first rain of the season yesterday! We measured 1-1/4" at home nearby. The reddish-brown color is California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum ssp. foliosum). (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b01b_3b01-122.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Wild Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) with the western summits of the Santa Ynez Mountains in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b01c_3b01-070.jpg
Notes: Glandular leaves and stems of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b01d_3b01-085.jpg
Notes: Next year's acorns on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). These acorns mature in one year. This year's acorn crop is pretty meager in our mountains. It will be a tough year for wildlife. I don't find many new acorns either. I hope this isn't a pattern. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b01e_3b01-093.jpg
Notes: Shiny new leaves of Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) look like sea creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b01f_3b01-078.jpg
Notes: Seed heads of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) on a fire break being reclaimed by chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b05a_3b03-230.jpg
Notes: There's a small universe of life on this Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) twig in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma. Lichens are a symbiotic partnership between fungi and algae, but only the Ascomycete fungal partners make these pretty fruiting bodies, encouraged by our recent first rain of the season. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b05b_3b03-212.jpg
Notes: Pretty insect gall on the underside of a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) leaf. I believe these are caused by the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus). These tumor-like growths are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) that lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 November 2003)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Crystalline Gall Wasp; Gall Wasps

File.Name: 3b05c_3b03-216.jpg
Notes: Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) stump-sprouting after a small wildfire two years ago. There is life after fire, our native chaparral plants are adapted for it. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b06a_3b03-263.jpg
Notes: Dandelion-like seeds of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) take to the wind. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 November 2003) These are seeds not flowers, but I still think of this quote from Anais Nin: "It takes courage to push yourself to places that you have never been before... to test your limits... to break through barriers. And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom.

File.Name: 3b06b_3b03-261.jpg
Notes: Dandelion-like seeds of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) about to take to the wind. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b06c_3b03-258.jpg
Notes: White Flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) bursting into seed. It looks like a snowman with the sun behind. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b06d_3b03-232.jpg
Notes: White Flowers of a female Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea). Those tufts of white hairs that make up the flowers will become the wings of the dandelion-like seeds that will soon emerge. This native composite shrub grows in waste places and is often dismissed as a weed. Coyote Bush one of my humble favorites. It flowers at an unlikely time and supports much life. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b07a_3b06-308.jpg
Notes: Reflections of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophylum) leaves in a small pool up the creek behind Cold Springs Tavern. It was very dark in the canyon this afternoon, but this photo managed to find light and focus from above. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b07b_3b07-378.jpg
Notes: This is my view driving home from school. The late afternoon light today was especially beautiful and worth a stop. Note the long shadows of the oak trees. This is looking north towards Cachuma Saddle and the Santa Barbara backcountry. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b07c_3b07-380.jpg
Notes: Clouds building over Lake Cachuma (really a reservoir) and the distant Solomon hills to the west. The late afternoon light today was very beautiful and worth a stop. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08a_3b08-061.jpg
Notes: These new flowers on Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) first appeared today, a week after our first rain of the season. How do they know? It was dark and drizzly when I took this photo without flash. Image quality aside, this is important because it`s the very first wildflower of the new season that I`ve seen. May there be many more! (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08b_3b08-014.jpg
Notes: LBMs ("Little Brown Mushrooms") growing on oak wood chips at our house. They are probably some species of Mycena. Note how the tiny 'shrooms are glued together by raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08c_3b08-044.jpg
Notes: Water drops and tendrils on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus. It was drizzling today when I took these photos - and now it's raining - but only a few plants catch the raindrops like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08d_3b08-051.jpg
Notes: The last berries on California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). The distinctive upper leaves are connate and grow together around the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08e_3b08-035.jpg
Notes: Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa), Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), and California Bay (Umbellularia californica) leaves on sandstone along our creek. Even the rock is turning color after recent rain. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)
CommonNames: Western Sycamore; Coast Live Oak; California Bay
ScientificName: Platanus racemosa; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Platanus racemosa; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 3b08f_3b08-025.jpg
Notes: Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves floating and soaking in a little pool of tea-colored water on our mostly-dry creek. We had our first rain a week ago, and these leaves have been steeping in rain water since then. The brown color is tannic acid leaching from the leaves - just like real tea. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08g_3b08-014.jpg
Notes: Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius) is such a perfect name for this interesting mushroom growing up through the hard edge of our road. Sometimes they come up through the road! The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a dye for colors ranging from gold to blue depending on the mordant. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b08h_3b08-020.jpg
Notes: LBMs ("Little Brown Mushrooms") growing on oak wood chips at our house. They are probably some species of Mycena. Note how the tiny 'shrooms are glued together by raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b09a_3b09-063.jpg
Notes: This old leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) is about to go. It looks like someone used a marker to circle the defects. Keep him away from me! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b09b_3b09-067.jpg
Notes: Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) leaf ensnared by a tendril of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus. Which one brought it down? (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b09c_3b09-070.jpg
Notes: These pretty flowers of the composite Cudweed-Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia are some of our last summer flowers. A new generation of flowers is starting with the recent rain. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b09d_3b08-041.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) growing over sandstone in our mostly dry creek. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b09e_3b08-046.jpg
Notes: Water drops and tendrils on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus). It was drizzling today when I took these photos - and now it's raining - but only a few plants catch the raindrops like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b09f_3b08-004.jpg
Notes: Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius) is such a perfect name for this interesting mushroom growing up through the hard gravel in our driveway. The fruiting body is made up of thousands of lentil-like chambers (peridioles) that are starting to break up here to release spores. You can see some color if you look close. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b10a_3b10-104.jpg
Notes: Leaves of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) starting to turn color. (Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b10b_3b10-091.jpg
Notes: Leaves on Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) starting to turn color. The way they dance in the wind is even better. (Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b10c_3b10-106.jpg
Notes: Corkscrew seedpods on Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum). Many plants in the pea family (Fabaceae) twist like this to squeeze out the seeds. This shrub has pretty flowers and interesting seed pods, but it`s a dangerous invasive weed. (Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b10d_3b10-077.jpg
Notes: Ripe gourd of Calabazilla (Cucurbita foetidissima). The species name tells how it tastes. The orange-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, tools, and rattles. It`s not common. This colony near Paradise Camp is huge, covering 1/2 acre or more. I wonder if it goes back to the old days. (Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b10e_3b10-100.jpg
Notes: I found this dead crawdad is a small pool along our drying up river. It really is an "organic soup"! (Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b10f_3b10-101.jpg
Notes: Odd geometry of green algae in an almost-dry pool on the river. Why does it curl up in this pattern? (Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b11a_3b11-121.jpg
Notes: We had dramatic clouds at school today, with "God light" - aka "crepuscular rays". I have a stumper about this phenomenon at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/24jan03.html. The real stumper is how to photograph these sunbeams. I took lots of pictures trying different combinations. For this photo (I think) I spot-metered off the sunbeams, and lowered the exposure two full stops. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b12a_3b12-196.jpg
Notes: We had more dramatic weather today, beautiful clouds with lightning and some rain. What struck me is the fractal similarity between the clouds and the trees in front. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b13a_3b13-243.jpg
Notes: The ruins of Knapp's Castle and the Santa Ynez Valley beyond. "The castle" is the ruin of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys remain. We had great views today after the dramatic weather and rain this week! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b13b_3b13-224.jpg
Notes: Spiny branches of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the rich afternoon light today. You can see next year's buds forming in the axils ("upper armpits") of the spines. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b13c_3b13-270.jpg
Notes: Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) flowers blowing in the wind - and probably spreading seeds too. It's a pretty introduced grass with a native "look" to it, but it's an invasive weed that's becoming all too common in the backcountry. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b14a_3b14-348.jpg
Notes: Ripening berries of Toyon or Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia) caught in the perfect light. This shrub is the "California Holly" that is the namesake for Hollywood. The late afternoon sun is coming from the left under a bank of storm clouds, and there are more dark clouds behind. This is straight from my camera. I tried adjusting color and contrast, but this is best. I have a stumper about "the perfect light" on my Stumper page at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/08feb02.html (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b14b_3b14-355.jpg
Notes: Trunk of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) with yellow lichens and exuding sap. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b14c_3b14-342.jpg
Notes: Ripe seed heads of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the late afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b14d_3b14-329.jpg
Notes: Looking west past a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) towards ridge after ridge of the Santa Ynez Mountains late in the afternoon. I live down below that first ridge. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15a_3b15-019.jpg
Notes: This seed pod of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) along the creek is full of wafer-like seeds. Note how the seeds are held in place by that elegant fiber net between the segments of the pod. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15b_3b15-002.jpg
Notes: LBM ("Little Brown Mushroom") growing on wood chips in the garden with Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). It is probably some species of Mycena. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15c_3b15-015.jpg
Notes: Jaws! I think these are Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) mushrooms growing on a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and engulfing an oak leaf from each side. This shows how fast everything is changing in the woods since our first rain on Halloween. It's raining now. Our woods are coming alive! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15d_3b15-038.jpg
Notes: Looks like a strange animal hoof, but it's just the rooting tip of a California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) stem growing up through a decomposing oak log. Brambles form because blackberries root at the tips of their stems. That's a hint for starting cuttings - plant them upside-down! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15e_3b15-011.jpg
Notes: New growth of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) and twists of shed Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) bark on a sandstone outcrop. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15f_3b15-008.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a California Polypody (Polypodium californicum fern just starting to grow from it's fertilized prothallus. Fern sex is complicated. Mature diploid ferns produce spores which become the small heart-shaped haploid prothallii. These produce mobile sperm that swim over wet surfaces like this rock near home to mate and produce new diploid ferns for next year. It's never easy! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15g_3b13-210.jpg
Notes: Crampball (Daldinia grandis), but I usually call it the charcoal 'shroom because that's what it looks and feels like. This one is growing from a cut oak log. It looks like it's oozing, but it's _v_e_r_y__s_l_o_w_ and it's as hard as a charcoal briquet. This fungus or something like it has been conected to "Sudden Oak Death" (SOD), see http://www.suddenoakdeath.org/ (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15h_3b13-207.jpg
Notes: Smooth Lepiota (Leucoagaricus naucinus aka Lepiota naucina) in the grass on the soccor field at school. David Arora calls it "Woman on Motorcycle" and these young caps DO look like helmets. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b15i_3b08-043.jpg
Notes: Smooth Lepiota (Leucoagaricus naucinus aka Lepiota naucina) in the grass on the soccor field at school. "Leucoagaricus" - "white agaricus" - is a good name because this looks and smells like an Agaricus with free gills and a ring on the stem, but it has a white spore print. Said to be edible, but be careful because it resembles Amanita. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b16a_3b16-045.jpg
Notes: Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta) in our garden. That's an ugly name for this beautiful native succulent. We live on the shady north side of the mountains, and we only get about an hour of sun at this time of year. I was busy today, and I never got past the garden, but I did pick the right time to get out with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b16b_3b16-041.jpg
Notes: White Sage (Salvia apiana) in our garden, with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b16c_3b16-046.jpg
Notes: New Iris leaves in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b16d_3b16-048.jpg
Notes: Tiny LBM ("Little Brown Mushroom") in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b16e_3b15-040.jpg
Notes: Bracket Fungus (Coriolus hirsutus?) growing on oak wood at home. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b16f_3b15-004.jpg
Notes: At first I thought this was an orange wood fungus growing by the compost heap. Then I realized it's a moldy old crookneck squash from the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b17a_3b16-043.jpg
Notes: Our garden is winding down after light frost and little sun on our shady side of the mountains. I haven`t seen a butterfly or honeybee since Halloween, but there is still life in the garden. Here are pretty yellow aphids (some mature) on the garden Milkweed still beset by preditors. The ladybug is obvious, but there`s also a tiny black parasitic wasp visible on the leaf in the middle-left. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b17b_3b15-032.jpg
Notes: Leaves of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in a new pool on our creek, with reflections. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b17c_3b15-036.jpg
Notes: Leaves of a small Toyon or Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia) lost in the shade along our creek. Mature shrubs with some sun are ripening berries, but this one is under attack. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b17d_3b15-039.jpg
Notes: Tendrils of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) along my trail. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19a_3b19-105.jpg
Notes: Broad-leaved Cattail (Typha latifolia) exploding with seeds. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19b_3b19-123.jpg
Notes: Next year's bud on Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii). The leaves are starting to turn gold and drop for winter. Those orange bumps on the leaf in front might be galls of the Poplar Erineum Gall Mite (Phyllocoptes didelphis) or something similar. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2003)
CommonNames: Fremont Cottonwood; Poplar Erineum Gall Mite

File.Name: 3b19c_3b19-083.jpg
Notes: Leaves of a clump of Broad-leaved Cattail (Typha latifolia) are dying from the outside in. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19d_3b19-082.jpg
Notes: Leaves of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis?) turning color along the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19e_3b19-100.jpg
Notes: New leaves on Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) are surprising because they look so much like the Willows that are now losing their leaves for winter. I have a stumper about that at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/21nov97.html (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19f_3b19-072.jpg
Notes: We have conferences at school this week, but I managed to slip away to the river during an afternoon break. This is a beaver dam on the barely running Santa Ynez River. This is the perfect way to relieve stress during conferences! Today's photos are all around this pond. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19g_3b17-050.jpg
Notes: The white-trimmed gills of Poison Pie (Hebeloma crustuliniforme) or a close relative growing under introduced Aleppo Pine (Pinus halepensis) at school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b19h_3b17-051.jpg
Notes: Poison Pie (Hebeloma crustuliniforme) or a close relative growing under introduced Aleppo Pine (Pinus halepensis) at school. It is poisonous, but some critter has been interested! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b20a_3b20-179.jpg
Notes: Brilliant crustose, squamulose, and foliose lichens on a rock outcrop. (Figueroa Mountain, 20 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b20b_3b20-165.jpg
Notes: Brilliant lichens on a serpentine rock outcrop. (Figueroa Mountain, 20 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b20c_3b20-167.jpg
Notes: Colony of Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on an exposed rock covered with gray lichens. This is an ancient plant related to ferns not mosses. (Figueroa Mountain, 20 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b20d_3b20-188.jpg
Notes: A tangle of Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) hanging from a Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree. (Figueroa Mountain, 20 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b20e_3b20-187.jpg
Notes: Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) hanging from a Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree. It's often called "Spanish Moss", though it's not a moss. I favor the name Treebeard Lichen! (Figueroa Mountain, 20 November 2003)
CommonNames: Lace Lichen; Blue Oak; Treebeard Lichen

File.Name: 3b20f_3b20-157.jpg
Notes: I had a break from school conferences today, so I drove up Figueroa Mountain Road into the Santa Barbara backcountry to look for mushrooms (none) and lichens (lots) to photograph. The road goes past Michael Jackson`s Neverland Ranch where this media crowd was waiting to see if "the cat came back". (He didn't.) Lots of interesting electronics and cameras, but I kept driving to those beautiful mountains beyond. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b20g_3b17-068.jpg
Notes: David Arora calls this Mock Meadow Mushroom or Fool's Agaric ("Agaricus californicus"). This one looks like a "meadow muffin"! It's coming up in hard ground at school after Halloween rains, but the cap is splitting after the dry weather this week. It looks like a commercial mushroom, but it has a bad antiseptic-phenol smell, and you DON'T want to eat it. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b21a_3b21-210.jpg
Notes: Beautiful white berries of Common Snowberry (Symphoricarpos albus?) growing along Alisal Creek. These plants are taller than me with lots of berries. They are surely different from the low Nuttall's Snowberry aka Tripweed (S. mollis) that grows near home and has few fruit this year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos albus?) growing along Alisal Creek. These plants are taller than me with lots of berries. They are surely different from the low Nuttall's Snowberry aka Tripweed (S. mollis
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos albus?) growing along Alisal Creek. These plants are taller than me with lots of berries. They are surely different from the low Nuttall's Snowberry aka Tripweed (S. mollis

File.Name: 3b21b_3b21-232.jpg
Notes: Seed head and fall leaves of Creek Clematis (Clematis Ligusticifolia). (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b21c_3b21-229.jpg
Notes: Leafy stipule at the base of a leaf of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa). This tree is deciduous. These little leaf-like stipules seem to turn color and drop before the leaves. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22a_3b22-008.jpg
Notes: Needles of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the late afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22b_3b22-012.jpg
Notes: Old leaf of deciduous Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) about to drop. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22c_3b22-013.jpg
Notes: Small Puffballs (Lycoperdon pusillum or kin) along the trail. Said to be edible, and some critter has nibbled the one on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22d_3b21-202.jpg
Notes: Rose hips on California Wild Rose (Rosa californica). (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22e_3b21-219.jpg
Notes: Pinnate leaves of Creek Clematis (Clematis Ligusticifolia) with a filagree of spider web. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22f_3b21-239.jpg
Notes: Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) bursting with seeds. How can all that fluff fit in such a slender pod? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22g_3b22-038.jpg
Notes: Our first fire of the winter season. It's cold tonight! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b22h_3b21-236.jpg
Notes: Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) bearding a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree showing some fall colors. Of course I favor the name Treebeard Lichen! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2003)
CommonNames: Lace Lichen; Western Sycamore; Treebeard Lichen

File.Name: 3b23a_3b23-053.jpg
Notes: Beautiful dying leaf of California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). I have a stumper about such "beautiful death" at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/03nov00.html (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b23b_3b23-050.jpg
Notes: Leaflets of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) catchng the afternoon light just right. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b23c_3b23-059.jpg
Notes: Spider webs on native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus). (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b23d_3b23-044.jpg
Notes: Spider webs on California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). There seem to be spider webs *everywhere* when I look close, but it takes the right light to make them visible. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b23e_3b23-063.jpg
Notes: Lichens on an oak branch with afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b23f_3b22-015.jpg
Notes: Julie sitting "on top of the world" with Santa Barbara below and Santa Cruz Island beyond. We call this the "Adventure Pass Trail" because the Forest Service put up an expensive display claiming this old trail and explaining how it was paid for with Adventure Pass funds. That's a fee we're supposed to pay to visit our own National Forest in our own backyard. Don't support this program! See http://www.freeourforests.org/ (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b24a_3b22-003.jpg
Notes: I'm on vacation for a week, yahoo! It`s too gloomy for photos today, so I`ve been cleaning my computer and house for Thanksgiving. If it clears tomorrow, Julie and I are heading into the backcountry for a day or two, upriver from this view. Those are native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) in the foreground. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b24b_3a05-021.jpg
Notes: My Mom left an aluminum pan on the stove too long, and it melted and dripped into this form that sure looks like a conversation! I have it on my shelf of priceless things. I dedicate this photo to a lady who welcomed me on my very first fotolog post. She has her garden, and I have my "San Marcos Pass". But she sets the standard for looking close at the familiar and making it wonderful and strange every day. I don't leave enough comments on her f'log, but I always look. Before I post any photo, I ask in the back of my mind: "Is this good enough for mollymolly?" Thanks, Molly. http://www.fotolog.net/mollymolly
Locality: My Mom left an aluminum pan on the stove too long, and it melted and dripped into this form that sure looks like a conversation!  I have it on my shelf of priceless things.  I dedicate this photo to a lady who welcomed me on my very first fotolog post.  She has her garden, and I have my "San Marcos Pass".  But she sets the standard for looking close at the familiar and making it wonderful and strange every day.  I don't leave enough comments on her f'log, but I always look.  Before I post any photo, I ask in the back of my mind: "Is this good enough for mollymolly?"  Thanks, Molly.  http://www.fotolog.net/mollymolly

File.Name: 3b25a_3b25-105.jpg
Notes: These elegant seed capsules of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) still have a few seeds left in the pods. This is natural afternoon light with a beam of sunlight in just the right place. That's what I look for here on the shady side of the mountains at this time of year! We planned to hike into the backcountry today, but stuff happens. I did manage a late hike up to the ridge to get a few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b25b_3b25-071.jpg
Notes: New grass coming up through the Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) needles on the ridge above my home. We had our first rain on Halloween, but now it's drying out. It's an interesting adaptation that the natives usually don't sprout on the first rain, but introduced weeds like these annual grasses do. Alas, the weeds have reserve seeds that will sprout later if the first batch fails. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b25c_3b25-098.jpg
Notes: Bundle of pine needles from Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) caught on a branch. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b25d_3b25-076.jpg
Notes: The gnarly scales on this old cone of Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) make me think about birds. The pinecones are massive. Don`t camp under these trees! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b25e_3b25-095.jpg
Notes: New catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) getting ready to open. That's Santa Barbara and the ocean in the distance. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b25f_3b25-112.jpg
Notes: Talk about survivors! This California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. canum) is growing from a crack in the sandstone and still flowering. The Hummingbirds must be grateful. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b25g_3b25-025.jpg
Notes: Lichens on a Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) twig. I'm really curious why some species harbor more lichens that others! We had good rain at Halloween, but it's getting dry again here in central California. The mushrooms are waiting. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b26a_3b26-135.jpg
Notes: We do have fall color here in central California, and few natives are as brilliant as Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). Enjoy, don't gather! (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b26b_3b26-126.jpg
Notes: New growth on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) looks almost like a flower. This native shrub is stump-sprouting from a burl after being cut to the ground during some trail work. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b26c_3b26-121.jpg
Notes: New leaves on a stump-sprouting Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis). I like the red trim on the leaf margins. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b26d_3b26-141.jpg
Notes: Almost ripe berries of Toyon or Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia), and lots more in the background. Toyon is doing well in our mountains this year, though there are very few acorns. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b26e_3b26-130.jpg
Notes: The waxy buds of Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) are beautiful, but I really like the smooth curves of the thick leaves. Sometimes it`s called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b26f_3b26-131.jpg
Notes: This unusual oak with shiny entire leaves is either Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or
Locality: This unusual oak with shiny entire leaves is either Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or

File.Name: 3b27a_2b23-003.jpg
Notes: Interior Live Oak (Q. wislizenii var. wislizenii). Either way it`s interesting. There`s a single grove of these small oak trees along West Camino Cielo road a few miles from the pass. (Anywhere else?) (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b28a_3b28-146.jpg
Notes: A good harvest of acorns from Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) is something for all critters to be thankful for. This photo is from a year ago. Acorns are scarce this year, and I think it will be a tough winter for wildlife. By tradition, we celebrate our family Thanksgiving dinner a day later on Friday. I`ll have a photo tomorrow. I wish all the fotolog community a good harvest and much to be thankful for! (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 November 2002) See my Forgotten Harvest stumper (and answer) at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/22nov02.html for more info about acorns and other holiday foods!

File.Name: 3b30a_3b30-015.jpg
Notes: Setting the table for Thanksgiving - which we always celebrate a day later on Friday. We had 20+ family and friends as dinner guests last night. I borrowed my dedication from Meister Eckhart: "If the only prayer you said in your whole life was 'thank you,' that would suffice." Thank you. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b30b_3b30-016.jpg
Notes: Shiny new growth on Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum). The bristly stems between the large spines at the leaf nodes is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3b30c_3b30-014.jpg
Notes: New growth on Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum). (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3c02a_3b30-026.jpg
Notes: A bare branch of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) outlined with spider webs. There are new leaves on the branch in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3c02b_3b30-024.jpg
Notes: Earthstars (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Despite the green grass seedlings, it's been dry and crunchy for a few weeks and mushrooms are scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3c02c_3b30-018.jpg
Notes: Unknown succulent (Crassulaceae) growing in a pot on our deck at home in afternoon light. Are those tendrils at the base of the stem hidden inside the juicy leaves? (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3c02d_3b30-009.jpg
Notes: Seed heads of California Brickellbush (Brickellia Californica). This is a late-flowering native subshrub that grows in disturbed places like this road cut. It's another unappreciated composite with dandelion-like seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3c03a_3c01-047.jpg
Notes: Seeds heads of Plummer's Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae ssp. plummerae). This late-flowering endemic subshrub is said to be uncommon, but it is easy to find near my home. It's an old naturalist insight that "rare" really means *rarely common*. This is another unappreciated composite with dandelion-like seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2003)

File.Name: 3c03b_3c01-049.jpg
Notes: Part of a large clump of Inky Cap (Coprinus atramentarius) mushrooms at the base of a Willow (Salix) tree. They've been coming up under this same tree for years. These mushrooms literally digest themselves into a black inky mess. They are said to be edible, but they can react with alcohol in a bad way. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c03c_3c01-031.jpg
Notes: Hard frost in the school garden. This pepper was one of the few that survived. It's time to plan for next year! (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c03d_3c01-037.jpg
Notes: Hard frost in the school garden. These peppers didn't make it. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c04a_3c03-066.jpg
Notes: A large clump of Inky Cap (Coprinus atramentarius) mushrooms pushing up through the gravel at the base of a willow (Salix) tree at my school. These are a pretty irresistible target at a middle school, but so far so good! (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c04b_3c03-060.jpg
Notes: This deliquescing (becoming inky) mushroom is part of a huge clump of Inky Caps (Coprinus atramentarius). These mushrooms literally digest themselves and turn into an inky black mess that you can write messages with. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c04c_3c03-063.jpg
Notes: This deliquescing (becoming inky) mushroom is part of a huge clump of Inky Caps (Coprinus atramentarius). These mushrooms literally digest themselves and turn into an inky black mess that you can write messages with. The gills of this specimen have dissolved just enough to pass the light. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c04d_3c03-061.jpg
Notes: The dissolving cap of this Inky Cap (Coprinus atramentarius) mushroom is twisted like chocolate curls. Looks can be deceiving with mushrooms! (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c04e_3c03-064.jpg
Notes: This deliquescing (becoming inky) mushroom is part of a huge clump of Inky Caps (Coprinus atramentarius). It has captured a willow tree from it's host tree. These mushrooms literally digest themselves and turn into an inky black mess that you can write messages with. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c05a_3c05-242.jpg
Notes: This young mushroom is part of a large clump of Inky Caps (Coprinus atramentarius) fruiting at the base of a Willow (Salix) tree. They've been coming up under this same tree for years. They are said to be edible when young, but they soon turn into an inky black mess. I've never tried eating them. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c05b_3c05-231.jpg
Notes: I was at the right place at the right time for a beautiful sunset tonight. All of today's photos are looking across Goleta and the ocean beyond from the "Old Pass Road". These photos are all straight from my camera except for some cropping. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c05c_3c05-239.jpg
Notes: Amazing sunset tonight! This is looking over the city of Goleta and the wine-red ocean beyond. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c05d_3c05-233.jpg
Notes: Amazing sunset tonight! I'm looking over the Goleta foothills and the ocean beyond. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c05e_3c05-218.jpg
Notes: I was at the right place at the right time for a beautiful sunset tonight. The sun is sinking off to the left, and there is a hint of "God-light" under these clouds over the Santa Ynez Mountains and the ocean beyond. The show is just beginning! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c06a_3c06-002.jpg
Notes: Lichens and mosses and fungi and ferns and liverworts, oh my! Here is some of the life in the micro-world on a small sandstone outcrop in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c06b_3c06-019.jpg
Notes: Fall leaves on a small Apple Tree (Malus pumila) at home. This tree started as a seedling from a tossed apple core when we built our house 20+ years ago. We get a few fruit every year if the Scrub Jays don`t knock them all down. I shot this photo looking up at the overcast sky today, and over-exposed a full stop to bring out some detail from the shadow. It`s a gloomy day and there`s a chance of much needed rain. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c06c_3c06-007.jpg
Notes: Sporangia of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta), with a fine filagree of spider webs. You have to get flat and look up at the underside of fern fronds to see these beautiful structures. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c06d_3c06-021.jpg
Notes: New buds of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) about to open. Note the tiny wasp on the leaf in the upper right. It's interesting that many of our natives shut down for winter just as others are starting to grow. I have a "Fall or Spring?" stumper about this at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/21nov97.html. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c06e_3c06-028.jpg
Notes: Autumn leaves of deciduous Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivus) bring color to the woods, even on a gloomy day like today. This is one of my favorite native shrubs in all seasons. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07a_3c07-043.jpg
Notes: The first real mushroom I've found this year at home. I think it's a Tricholoma, maybe White Tricholoma (Tricholoma resplendens) or kin. Those tiny dark specks on the rim of the cap are springtails. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07b_3c07-088.jpg
Notes: Looking under the skirt of the first real mushroom I've found this year at home, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). White spores, notched or sinuate gills, not waxy, no veil, slightly viscid - I think it's a Tricholoma, maybe White Tricholoma (Tricholoma resplendens) or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; sinuate; Tricholoma; Tricholoma resplendens
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; sinuate; Tricholoma; Tricholoma resplendens

File.Name: 3c07c_3c07-077.jpg
Notes: Raindrops hanging like Christmas lights on a complicated spider web. Is that the spider's home in the center? We didn't get much rain from todays welcome storm, but the drops hanging on leaves and webs are wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07d_3c07-085.jpg
Notes: Raindrops caught on a Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae) web built around a California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf. It looks like the leaf is swimming in bubbles. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07e_3c07-093.jpg
Notes: Raindrops caught on the web of a Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae) after todays' drizzle. I can't explain the amazing reflections. The web looks like it's curving away, but it is really strung like a hammock coming back up towards the camera on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07f_3c07-057.jpg
Notes: A single flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) caught in a spider web, with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07g_3c07-069.jpg
Notes: Raindrops become lenses on a leaf of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus). (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c07h_3c07-102.jpg
Notes: New Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) leaf and tendrils holding drops from today's drizzle. The reddish-brown blob to the right is California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). You can see it reflected in the lower drops on the sweetpea. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c08a_3c07-097.jpg
Notes: Another shot of the same spider web as the previous photo. I took this a couple of hours later when most of the drops had fallen away to reveal the chaotic structure of the web in fresh light. We got less than 1/10th inch of rain from that storm, but a real rain would have knocked down the web and the drops. Whatever happens, there`s a photo in it! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c09a_3c07-100.jpg
Notes: Last years' Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) growing on old firewood. This common Polypore or "bracket fungus" is so tough it still looks fresh after a year in the sun. New growth is on the way after recent rain! (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c09b_3c06-038.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on new growth of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus). But look closer - many of those new leaves have legs! I didn`t notice all those leaf-green aphids until I looked at the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c09c_3c07-115.jpg
Notes: New leaves on a Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) seedling. The pinnate real leaves are very different from the first spade-shaped cotyledon leaves at the base. Spring wildflowers are beginning! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c09d_3c06-025.jpg
Notes: Bright fall colors of a small Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree on the lip of a dry waterfall on our creek. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c10a_3c10-123.jpg
Notes: Dramatic storm clouds at school today. This is looking south at the Santa Ynez Mountains and the sun off to the right. Not much rain, but more is on the way. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c10b_3c10-126.jpg
Notes: This is the notch in the dramatic storm clouds in the previous photo. Light is spilling through like water over a dam. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c10c_3c10-129.jpg
Notes: Dramatic storm clouds at school today. Not much rain, but more is on the way. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c11a_3c10-138.jpg
Notes: Looking up into the canopy of a Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) after today's storm. This particular tree is an Acorn Woodpecker (Melanerpes formicivorus) granary. You can see a few nest holes on the big trunk under the doughnut-shaped scar. These beautiful oaks are deciduous, but they don't make a show of it. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c12a_3c12-305.jpg
Notes: Golden leaves of deciduous Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) as the evening shadow starts to rise. Most of my drive home after school is now in shadow. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c12b_3c12-310.jpg
Notes: This Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in late afternoon light has lost all but a few leaves. This is in the ranch land on my way home from school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13a_3c13-079.jpg
Notes: Moss and lichens on a shaded sandstone outcrop. We've had drizzle, but I'm still waiting for real rain to bring up the mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13b_3c13-051.jpg
Notes: Amber in the making? This is pitch dripping from a Knobcone Pine (Pinus attenuata) caught with (dim) natural light. These mature pines near home appear native, but I believe they were planted by the Forest Service early in the last century. This particular tree was exuding a huge amount of pitch. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13c_3c13-061.jpg
Notes: Impressive prickers and shiny new leaves of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum). The bristly stems between the large spines at the leaf nodes is typical. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13d_3c13-020.jpg
Notes: Leaves of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivus) framed by sandstone and chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13e_3c13-008.jpg
Notes: The rugged Santa Ynez Valley foothills of Figueroa Mountain, with a floral frame of ripe berries on Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13f_3c13-037.jpg
Notes: Beautiful catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) with ripe berries of Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the background. The catkins are not quite open yet. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c13g_3c13-058.jpg
Notes: Umbellate seed head of last summer's Golden Stars (Bloomeria crocea). There are still a few black seeds in the pods. I collected a few for the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14a_3c13-028.jpg
Notes: Yellow lichens on branches of Knobcone Pine (Pinus attenuata). It's neat how the twigs spiral around the trunk, and you can see how the lichens try to avoid the direct sun off to the right. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14b_3c13-012.jpg
