Notes: Oaks have acorns instead of juicy fruit. These "oak apple" type insect galls on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) are something different. I believe these are caused by the California Gall Wasp (Andricus californicus), a species of tiny wasps known only from parthenogenic females. You can cut a gall open and find the hard capsules of several wasp larvae inside. The bottom gall is still succulent, but the upper one has become hard and dry. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14c_3c13-018.jpg
Notes: Oaks have acorns, so these must be bud or stem galls on Canyon Live Oak aka Goldencup Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). I'm sure these galls are caused by Cynipid gall wasps. This is an interesting native oak that grows as a shrub in the mountains but sometimes becomes a huge tree in the north-slope canyons. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14d_3c13-016.jpg
Notes: Hoary leaves of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) with nice backlight to show the structure of the leaves. This is our mainland form of a shrub mostly limited to Santa Catalina Island. It's named for Blanche Trask, one of the most interesting early California naturalists. Try a web search to learn more about this fascinating lady. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14e_3c13-043.jpg
Notes: Shredding bark of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14f_3c13-072.jpg
Notes: Soft flower of Rabbitsfoot Grass (Polypogon monspeliensis) growing on the dry creekbed of the upper reach of Paradise Creek. Somehow this introduced grass got to this remote canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c14g_3c13-084.jpg
Notes: More amber in the making. Here is lots more pitch dripping from a Knobcone Pine (Pinus attenuata) caught with flash on the shady side of the tree. It doesn't have the rich sunset color of the [previous] photo, but the blues are interesting. It's a child's idea of frosting! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15a_3c14-154.jpg
Notes: This boat was part of the Santa Barbara "Parade of Lights" in the harbor with boats dressed up with lights like Christmas Trees. It's not an explosion. They set off a bunch of very bright flares at they moved past! (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15b_3c14-149.jpg
Notes: And now for something completely different... I rarely leave home on the weekends except for a good hike, but we received an invitation from a friend on the Santa Barbara Harbor Commission to help judge the "Parade of Lights" at the Santa Barbara Harbor. The boats were dressed up with lights like Christmas Trees. Great fun, but a hopeless photo situation. I had my tripod, but we were on the crowded VIP deck with lots of people moving and cheering; and the boats were parading by; and I needed 1+ second exposures. I made the best of it! (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15c_3c14-143.jpg
Notes: The Santa Barbara "Parade of Lights" with boats dressed up with lights like Christmas Trees in the harbor. I tried to follow this boat by panning my camera as it went by during a long exposure, so the background lights are streched into bumpy lines. (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15d_3c14-128.jpg
Notes: The Santa Barbara "Parade of Lights" with boats dressed up with lights like Christmas Trees in the harbor. I kept my camera still on my tripod in this photo, but the decorated boat is moving past. You can see more boats in the "Parade of Lights" approaching, and city lights bryond. (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15e_3c14-112.jpg
Notes: Looking back towards Santa Barbara from Stern's Wharf in the harbor. The bright lights on the shore are from the Cabrillo Arts Center. (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15f_3c14-118.jpg
Notes: Sunset in the harbor. The sun has gone down for me, but the tall masts of the boats are still catching the last rays. (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c15g_3c14-139.jpg
Notes: I rarely leave home on the weekends except for a good hike, but we got an invitation from a friend on the Santa Barbara Harbor Commission to help him judge the "Parade of Lights" at the Santa Barbara Harbor. What fun! Here's a shot from the wharf looking back at home on San Marcos Pass, the low point in the mountains between the palm trees. It was raining in the morning, but these pretty clouds are the only reminder at dusk. (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c16a_3c14-169.jpg
Notes: Here's one more from the Santa Barbara harbor on Sunday night. This is looking across the harbor at the pier after the parade. (Santa Barbara, 14 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c19a_3c19-403.jpg
Notes: There's another world in the woods when you get down on your belly and look close! That's a pencil point for comparison. This tiny 'shroom is probably the same species of Mycena as the [previous] photo. What else are we missing? (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c19b_3c19-404.jpg
Notes: LBMs ("Little Brown Mushrooms") growing on oak wood chips at our house. They are probably some species of Mycena. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c19c_3c19-405.jpg
Notes: New growth of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens?) growing under the oaks. Fall/winter is two-faced here in the central California mountains. Many plants are shutting down just as others are starting to grow. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c19d_3c19-412.jpg
Notes: New leaves of Wood Rush (Luzula comosa) growing under the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c19e_3c19-416.jpg
Notes: Intersecting light and shadow of leaves of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus). (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c19f_3c19-428.jpg
Notes: First day of holiday vacation from school. It was sunny when I left home on my walk, but the clouds thickened and now we're getting some drops. We need it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20a_3c20-059.jpg
Notes: I found our first Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom on my walk today! I looked high and low, and only found this one. The blunt gills are distinctive. Edible and choice, and soon to be part of dinner! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20b_3c20-029.jpg
Notes: Little Brown Mushroom (LBM) growing along our road. It has an almost-black spore print, maybe Psathyrella? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20c_3c20-034.jpg
Notes: Here is the first Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom I've found this year. May there be many more! Clean and slice your chanterelle into a hot frying pan with a pinch of salt but no oil or butter and let it stew in its own juices, stirring constantly. After the liquid evaporates, add butter and garlic and fresh pepper and keep stirring. This is as good as it gets! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20d_3c20-048.jpg
Notes: I found our first Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom on my walk today! I looked high and low, and only found this one. The blunt gills are distinctive. Edible and choice, and soon to be part of dinner! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20e_3c20-005.jpg
Notes: I found the first Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom on my walk today! This photo is looking up under the cap at the distinctive blunt fold-like gills that are different from the sharp blade-like gills of most agarics. Edible and choice. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20f_3c20-039.jpg
Notes: Little Brown Mushroom (LBM) growing along our road. It has an almost-black spore print, maybe Psathyrella? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20g_3c20-026.jpg
Notes: Moldy mushroom is NOT an oxymoron! This mushroom (maybe the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus) or kin?) is growing on a dead oak branch on the ground, but it has been taken over by a fluffy white mold. You can barely make out the host mushroom's gills. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c20h_3c20-047.jpg
Notes: Gloomy weather today with a few sprinkles, but it's good for the mushrooms! I don't know what these pretty little mushrooms are that are coming up in the woods near home. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21a_3c21-112.jpg
Notes: Tiny little 'shroom growing in the woods with moss and seedling wildflowers. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21b_3c21-064.jpg
Notes: The first blushing flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). Look close on the top stem and you`ll find a dandelion-like composite seed that got stuck on this reef. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21c_3c21-078.jpg
Notes: This beautiful flower was a surprise today! Several plants are just starting to flower on the bare ground on top of a recent landslide along our mountain road. I believe this is California Blue-bell (Phacelia campanularia) which is native to the California desert but not our area. Probably it came from a seed packet, but there are no other unusual plants here. Local wildflowers are hard enough to identify (especially Phacelias!) without these visitors. But it is beautiful and doing well. I will collect seeds for the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21d_3c21-105.jpg
Notes: Ripe fruit on Toyon or Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia). There are few acorns this year, but at least the birds will feast. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21e_3c21-095.jpg
Notes: New flowers on Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21f_3c21-083.jpg
Notes: Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) grows for years, then flowers once and dies. But what a flower it is! This is looking close at the base of last summer's flower stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c21g_3c20-054.jpg
Notes: There are still flowers on Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora). This weedy native starts flowering on top and then works its way down. Those are Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2003)
ScientificName: Heterotheca grandiflora). This weedy native starts flowering on top and then works its way down. Those are Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Heterotheca grandiflora). This weedy native starts flowering on top and then works its way down. Those are Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri

File.Name: 3c22a_3c22-154.jpg
Notes: Liverworts (Marchantia?) getting ready to "flower" on damp soil near home. They look like little tongues with a treat! These moss relatives are part of the micro-world we rarely notice. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c22b_3c20-025.jpg
Notes: New fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern growing around a branch of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c22c_3c22-152.jpg
Notes: Pretty little fruticose lichen (Cladonia?) fruiting bodies (called "podetia") growing on damp soil near home. Each tiny cap holds a raindrop on top. This is pushing my macro capabilities to the limit. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c22d_3c22-128.jpg
Notes: Last years' Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) - or maybe this is the Silverback Fern variety (ssp. viscosa)? You can make pretty "fern tattoos" by pressing the spore-bearing leaves to your skin. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c22e_3c22-138.jpg
Notes: I found this inscription on a sandstone outcrop along the 100+ year old stagecoach road between Cold Springs Tavern and Paradise Store: "N.P. Hockinson Wahoo Mss." The first two lines look old judging from the dark lichens in the carving. The bottom line looks more recent and not as careful. The first two lines look old judging from the dark lichens in the carving. The bottom line looks more recent and not as careful. Then again I might be tricked by a recent graffiti. It`s pretty cool what you can find looking for mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c22f_3c22-139.jpg
Notes: This was exciting! On a hike in the canyon today between Cold Springs Tavern and Paradise Store, I found another section of the old stagecoach road over San Marcos Pass 100+ years ago. Here you can see a rut worn into the sandstone bedrock by the iron-rimmed wagon wheels. I didn't have much time to explore today, but I'll be back! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c22h_3c22-159.jpg
Notes: David Arora calls this the Nitrous Entoloma (Entoloma nidorosum or kin). I call it the "swimming pool shroom" because it has such a strong chlorine/bleach smell. It would never occur to me to eat it. (Don't!) It grows under our oaks and has "pink" or flesh-colored spores. This is the first one I've seen this season. All those white spots on the cap are tiny Springtail (Collembola) insects that leap (relatively) huge distances when you disturb them. Why would any mushroom smell like bleach? Does all this tiny life matter?? (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c23a_3c20-009.jpg
Notes: Seedlings of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata aka Montia perfoliata etc.) after recent rains. These plants only get the distinctive round leaves when they flower. We're not getting much rain so far this year, but it keeps coming. Yes, we felt the earthquake, but it was no big deal here. I was climbing up our shakey stairs at the moment and barely noticed it. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c23b_3c22-161.jpg
Notes: Leaves of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) catching the afternoon light just right after todays good rain. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c23c_3c23-181.jpg
Notes: Fog moving through the Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge above our home. This is looking the other way from the [previous] photo at about the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c23d_3c23-173.jpg
Notes: The sunset view from our porch tonight as the storm breaks after 3/4" of rain today. This is looking northwest towards Figueroa Mountain and Ranger Peak. That bright patch near Goat Rock was catching the light just right. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c23e_3c23-162.jpg
Notes: These are the first Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushrooms I've seen this year, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) on the edge of our garden. Said to be edible, but even the caps are tough and I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28a_3c28-058.jpg
Notes: Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) growing out between shelves of Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) growing on an old oak log. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2003)
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor

File.Name: 3c28b_3c27-002.jpg
Notes: Lots of Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms at my parents' house for Christmas! (Santa Monica Mountains, 27 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28c_3c27-021.jpg
Notes: I must have been shaky after the wine... There were Christmas lights on the table below, and I took a hand-held photo of the reflections on the bubble-skylight above. Sure does look like mushrooms! (26 December 2003)
Locality: I must have been shaky after the wine... There were Christmas lights on the table below, and I took a hand-held photo of the reflections on the bubble-skylight above.  Sure does look like mushrooms!  (26 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28d_3c27-028.jpg
Notes: Orchids and Ice - here are ice crystals on new leaves of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) at home after noon. The ground never did thaw today here in the mountains. The previous photo is an orchid flower at my parents' house 100+ miles to the south. Central California is a great place to be a naturalist! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28e_3c27-050.jpg
Notes: Orchids and Ice - This orchid is growing as an epiphyte on a tree fern in Grammy's garden 100+ miles south of my icy house in the Santa Barbara mountains. It's startling to see such a flower (with many more blooms) at this time of year. The "inner parts" look like a cartoon whale swimming out of the floral tube. (Santa Monica Mountains, 27 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28f_3c27-208.jpg
Notes: Remarkable Eucalyptus flowers in Grammy's garden. The flowers and massive seed pods are a couple of inches across. This tree is just a sapling, and we don't know the parent. (Santa Monica Mountains, 27 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28g_3c26-202.jpg
Notes: Pretty Composite flowers in Grammy's garden. This is looking through the petals towards the sun. (Santa Monica Mountains, 27 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28h_3c27-034.jpg
Notes: Camellia (related to tea) flowering for Christmas in Grammy's fabulous garden. (Santa Monica Mountains, 27 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c28i_3c28-069.jpg
Notes: We celebrated Christmas a day late (as usual) at my parents' house down south yesterday. This swan is just one of my Mom's original glass ornaments on the tree. Mom (aka Grammy) reminded me that I made that paper chain back in elementary school 45+ years ago! (Mothers are like that.) (Santa Monica Mountains, 27 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29a_3c29-119.jpg
Notes: A cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. These are poisonous. The greenish tones are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29b_3c29-115.jpg
Notes: Elegant Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushrooms coming up along a trail in the woods. These are starting to become common after the recent rain. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29c_3c29-094.jpg
Notes: Pretty Psathyrella longipes (or kin) mushroom in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Note the white veil fragments on the edge of the cap. The caps are large - a couple of inches across - and very brittle. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29d_3c29-088.jpg
Notes: The first Blewits (Clitocybe nuda) of the season! That brilliant purple color of the stalk is real, but the caps soon fade to brown or tan. Blewits are edible and common, almost a weed in disturbed places. May there be many more! Today was a dark gloomy day with a new storm threatening. Perfect for a mushroom walk! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29e_3c29-123.jpg
Notes: Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushrooms coming up along a trail in the woods. These are starting to become common after the recent rain. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29f_3c29-108.jpg
Notes: I know our local wildflowers pretty well, but I love stalking mushrooms because sometimes I don't have a clue! This mushroom is growing from the hollow center of an old firewood log left over from clearing along our road. At first I thought it was a pipe or wedge stuck in the log. I don't know what this is. (yet!) (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29g_3c29-113.jpg
Notes: I think this is a Tricholoma, maybe the White Tricholoma (Tricholoma resplendens) or kin. It's smaller than a rabbit hole, but it still looks like a doorway. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29h_3c29-117.jpg
Notes: Stately Psathyrella longipes (or kin) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c29i_3c29-120.jpg
Notes: A cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. These are poisonous, but they don't really look much like chanterelles. The greenish tones are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30a_3c30-164.jpg
Notes: When butter goes bad! This is Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) growing with shelves of Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) on an old oak log. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor

File.Name: 3c30b_3c30-171.jpg
Notes: I`m not sure what this mushroom is. It has a dark brown almost black cap and pink spores, but no veil or volva. It`s growing in the garden near dead wood, mostly in clusters. It has a "farinaceous" linseed oil smell, but not bleachlike. Maybe it is Pluteus cervinus or Entoloma rhodopolium. Whatever it is, it`s pretty coming up with the new grass. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30c_3c30-167.jpg
Notes: Seedling Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) coming up through oak and madrone leaves. The seedlings are distinctive. After the first two seed leaves or "cotyledons", they produce typical hand-like or "palmate" true leaves. Our summer wildflowers are starting life! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30d_3c30-150.jpg
Notes: Some Leaf Miner insect left this trail on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). Imagine being so tiny that a single leaf is your continent to explore. The ends of the trail must mark birth and emergence. I reckon we all leave such trails through space-time that maybe others will notice. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30e_3c30-137.jpg
Notes: Seedling of Bedstraw (Galium aparine) coming up through oak and madrone leaves. The seedlings are distinctive. After the first two seed leaves or "cotyledons", they grow with whorls of four leaves. Said to be introduced, but it's so common even in the backcountry that it's hard to believe it's not native. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30f_3c30-141.jpg
Notes: New seedlings coming up on the edge of the garden. I guess these are Chickweed (Stellaria media). (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30g_3c30-162.jpg
Notes: LBMs ("Little Brown Mushrooms") aren't always brown. These pretty little mushrooms are growing in the woods with the blackberrys under the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c30h_3c30-134.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Stalked Puffballs (Tulostoma brumale or kin?) near our garden. It's a puffball on a stalk with a nipple-like opening on top to release spores. Note how the cap has two layers. The rough outer cap (or peridium) is peeling away showing its fluffy white underside and smooth inner cap. I haven't seen these in the woods. I wonder if this was introduced with a nursery plant. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31a_3c31-180.jpg
Notes: Cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea and kin) starting to open on an oak stump at home. It's a big cluster. Even mushrooms can party for New Year's! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31b_3c31-178.jpg
Notes: We did a hike today through perfect oak woodland looking for Chanterelles. Nada. I did find a single Slippery Jack (Suillus pungens) growing under a planted Monterey Pine (Pinus radiata). The cap was shiny and dry, but you can tell it was viscid or slimy by the needles that are stuck there. Edible but bland. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31c_3c31-189.jpg
Notes: A cluster of Entoloma rhodopolium or kin. This is growing on the edge of our garden near a stump that is also sprouting Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea). It has a dark cap and pink spores. It smells "farinaceous" like linseed oil but not like bleach. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31d_3c31-185.jpg
Notes: Ladybird, ladybird, fly away home... This canyon in our mountains is where they fly to! All these Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) started the party early for New Year's Eve. There are many thousands of them in the canyon, and their party will go on for another month or two. I hope all you f'loggers are having this much fun! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31e_3c28-077.jpg
Notes: New Year's Eve for all these Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens). I hope you're having this much fun! That white thing with all the legs in the lower right is actually a sticky seed of the nasty weed Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis ssp. purpurea) which is all over this canyon. It took 15 minutes to clean our socks and our dog so we don't bring any seeds home. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31f_3c30-145.jpg
Notes: These interesting galls on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) leaves are probably caused by the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras trimaculosum). The detachable galls have stiff hairs like the caterpillar. I only found them on the midrib vein on the underside of the oak leaves. They are solitary, but when a few grow together like on top, they really do look like a woolybear! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31g_3c30-149.jpg
Notes: Large stem galls on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). I believe these massive galls are caused by unisexual generations of the Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola). They also have alternating sexual generations with completely different solitary galls on leaf buds. These woody galls are a permanent part of the stem. I don't see any exit (or entry) holes. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31h_3c31-192.jpg
Notes: This cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea and kin) is growing visibly day by day by an oak stump on the edge of our garden. Look close for the sowbug on the middle-right side. Caught between a rock and a hard place? Can mushrooms really grow that fast?! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2003)

File.Name: 3c31i_3c28-078.jpg
Notes: Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea and kin) is named for its color not taste. Gardeners have their own interests and call it "Oak Root Fungus" because it sometimes attacks many garden plants in California. I don't consider it a problem at home. They always grow on or near dead wood, sometimes buried. We have a several large clusters just beginning. This one is by itself in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2003)
